entries.title: ResearchOS Wiki
headings: First time here? Read this first.
Sharing a folder with your lab?
bodySnippets: Everything you need to set up ResearchOS, share a folder with your lab, and use each feature. Most users only need the first two sections, Getting Started and Shared Lab Accounts , to be productive.

ResearchOS is a single-page research-management app that reads and writes directly to a folder on your computer. There's no server, no account, no sign-up. Your data lives in plain JSON files you fully own.
Open ResearchOS in Chrome or Edge . Other browsers can't read local folders yet.

Click Link Folder to point ResearchOS at a folder on your disk. It can be an existing ResearchOS folder or a brand-new empty one you made first (Chrome cannot create a folder from the picker, so starting fresh means making an empty folder in your file manager, then linking it)...
Pick a username. Your data lives under inside that folder. You can set an optional password to gate access on a shared machine.
Once you sign in, you land on an empty account ready to fill. See Welcome Wizard for a walk through the major surfaces.
Start adding projects, methods, experiments, and Gantt tasks. Everything syncs to disk immediately.
The ? icon in the top-right of the app header always opens this wiki.
On the folder-connect screen, look for the New here? Read the setup guide link, which goes to the Connecting Your Folder page.
Use Ctrl + F / Cmd + F to search within a wiki page.

Looking for security and privacy details? The Security page covers data storage, encryption, and what never leaves your machine. It does not appear in the section grid above because it has no sub-pages.
The wiki is big because the app is big. Start Here covers what the app is, the few things worth knowing up front, and how to find your way around the rest of the wiki.

Read Shared Lab Accounts first. Putting the ResearchOS folder inside OneDrive, Google Drive, Dropbox, or iCloud lets multiple lab members use the same folder, but you have to configure it to stay downloaded locally . If you don't, ResearchOS won't be able to read and write rel...

bodySnippets: Plain-English answers to the two questions labs ask before they trust an electronic notebook with grant-funded data. Does ResearchOS support the NIH Data Management & Sharing Policy, and how does it compare to LabArchives?

entries.title: Depositing to a repository
headings: How this fits the NIH policy
One-click publishing is coming, not here yet
Where the funder data comes from
bodySnippets: When a funder asks you to share your data, you publish it to a public repository that mints a DOI. ResearchOS does the tedious part for you. It gathers your experiment into a download-ready bundle and prefills the repository's metadata form from the grant and ORCID details you...

A DOI (a permanent, citable link) is what turns a folder of files into shareable, findable research. Repositories like Zenodo and Figshare mint that DOI for you when you upload a dataset and fill in a metadata form. The form is the slow part, since it wants a title, an author ...

This is the most important thing to understand about the Deposit flow. ResearchOS never publishes anything on your behalf and never talks to a repository for you. It builds the bundle and the metadata locally, in your browser, and hands them to you. The final upload and the fi...

That means nothing leaves your machine until you decide to upload it, and nothing becomes public until you click Publish on the repository. There is no auto-publish, no background sync, and no surprise release of your data.
You can deposit a single experiment or a whole project.
The Deposit dialog walks through curation, metadata review, and handoff.

Curate. Choose which sections and attachments go in the bundle, and pick the bundle format (a self-contained HTML page, a PDF, or the raw files). This reuses the same export pipeline that powers a normal experiment export, so the bundle looks exactly like the rest of ResearchO...
Review the metadata. ResearchOS prefills a DataCite-shaped metadata record (the standard repositories speak) and shows it for you to check and edit. See the field list below.

Hand off. Pick Zenodo , Figshare , or Other repository . ResearchOS downloads one archive (named ) that holds your curated data and a metadata file, then opens the repository's own upload page in a new tab. For Zenodo and Figshare that button jumps you straight to their new-up...
The metadata step starts from what ResearchOS already knows, so you mostly confirm rather than type.

The deposit archive is a download. It is never written into your on-disk data folder and never becomes a new sidecar, so depositing leaves your project files untouched. Once the repository mints the DOI, copy it back into ResearchOS wherever you cite the dataset, and record it...
From an experiment : open the experiment's detail popup and click the Deposit to a repository button in its header. The bundle is that one experiment.

From a project : open the project and choose Deposit to bundle it as one dataset. The first step lets you multi-select which experiments and which notes go in (notes come from your full note list, since notes are not project-scoped). The bundle then holds each item exported on...
Title : the experiment title, or the project name for a project-level deposit.
Creator with ORCID : your display name, with your ORCID attached as a name identifier when you have one on file.
Abstract : seeded from your results, then notes, with the editing-history stamps stripped out. Fully editable.

License : the one field you must set before handoff, since NIH expects shared data to carry a license and ResearchOS has none to fall back on. The two NIH-friendly defaults (CC-BY-4.0 and CC0-1.0) are surfaced first as recommended.
Keywords : pulled from the experiment's tags.

Funder and award number : read from the grant linked to the project, including the funder name, the funder ID and ID type, and the award number and title. These populate the DataCite so the deposit carries the grant attribution funders expect. A project-level deposit goes furt...

Depositing to a public repository is how you satisfy the sharing half of the NIH Data Management and Sharing Policy. For the full picture of what the policy requires and how ResearchOS supports it, see NIH Data Management & Sharing .

A future Phase 2 will offer one-click browser-direct publishing to Zenodo, whose deposit API works straight from the browser. That is on the roadmap and is not shipped today. Everything described on this page is the guided path that ships now, where you finish the upload on th...

The funder fields come from the funding account linked to your project. You enter those structured grant details (award number, funder name, funder ID, award title) once in the funding-accounts manager, then link a project to that grant. See Purchases & Funding for how to set ...

The translation from your ResearchOS data into the DataCite record is a pure, unit-tested function. Its tests assert the title, the creator ORCID, the funding reference, and the graceful fallbacks when a piece is missing, so the field mapping you see in the dialog is verified,...
The metadata review step. Every field is prefilled from data you already entered and stays editable before you hand off.

The Deposit dialog on its metadata review step, showing the prefilled title, a creator row with an ORCID, an editable abstract, a license picker, keyword chips, and a funding reference block listing the funder name and award number.

entries.title: ResearchOS vs LabArchives
bodySnippets: An honest side-by-side. LabArchives is the incumbent most academic labs are leaving, so it is the right thing to measure against. ResearchOS wins decisively on data ownership and cost. LabArchives is still ahead on a few specific things, and this page says so plainly.

LabArchives is a hosted, cloud ELN sold per seat. You trade money and data custody for managed backups, security certifications, and turnkey institutional administration. It suits labs that want a vendor to hold the data and an IT department that wants a certified cloud contract.

ResearchOS is a free, open-source, local-first ELN. Your data is a folder of open files on your own disk, shared across a lab through whatever cloud drive you already pay for. It suits labs that want to own their data outright, avoid per-seat fees, and keep unpublished work of...
Saying otherwise would not survive a demo, so here it is straight.

You own your data, in open formats, forever. The single biggest difference. Your work is plain files on your disk, not rows in a vendor database. NIH's repository guidance prizes exactly this kind of portability and non-proprietary format.

No per-seat fees. A ten-person lab on LabArchives Professional is several thousand dollars a year, every year. ResearchOS has no per-seat fees and no paywalled features. Every feature is free, and the only optional paid part is cloud storage for a lab that uses a lot of it, pr...
Unpublished research never leaves your control. No vendor server holds your pre-publication data. For sensitive or competitive work, that is a real advantage, not a checkbox.
Built for bench work. The visual PCR editor, plate layouts, LC gradients, cell-culture schedules, Gantt scheduling, and lab purchasing are first-class, not bolt-ons.
Switching in is easy. ResearchOS imports your LabArchives Offline Notebook ZIP directly, so you are not retyping years of notebooks.

Fully automated one-click deposit. Built-in Figshare export and direct DOI minting from inside the app. ResearchOS has a guided deposit today (it prefills the metadata, bundles the data, and opens the repository's upload page where the DOI is minted), and the fully automated o...

Complete per-entry revision history. ResearchOS now ships per-entry version history with a restore button on notes, tasks, experiments (both Lab Notes and Results), projects, and sequences, and an experiment also tracks per-method protocol deviations as it changes, so the gap ...
Security certifications. FedRAMP, SOC 2, ISO 27001, and 21 CFR Part 11. If your institution mandates a certified cloud vendor, this is decisive, and ResearchOS does not compete here by design.
Managed backups and any-browser access. The vendor handles redundancy, and there is no File System Access API browser requirement.

If your priorities are owning your data in open formats, eliminating per-seat cost, and keeping unpublished work off a vendor server, ResearchOS is the better choice and supports your NIH DMS compliance fully (see NIH Data Management & Sharing ). If your institution requires a...

entries.title: NIH Data Management & Sharing
headings: There is no such thing as a certified ELN
What the policy actually asks for
Where your data actually gets shared
So what are universities actually paying for?
NIH does not want your lab notebook
Guided deposit now, one-click coming soon
Per-entry version history with restore ships today
Structured grant and ORCID fields feed the deposit
bodySnippets: Vendors advertise "NIH Data Management & Sharing support" like it is a certificate they bought. It is not. This page explains what the policy actually requires, why no electronic notebook is "NIH certified," and exactly how ResearchOS supports your compliance.

The NIH Data Management and Sharing (DMS) Policy, in effect since January 25 2023, binds researchers and institutions that receive NIH funding. It does not bind software. There is no NIH program that audits, licenses, or certifies an electronic lab notebook, and there is no ba...

Compliance is something a researcher does, not something a tool is. You comply by writing a Data Management and Sharing Plan with your grant application and then following it. The notebook is where the work that the plan describes actually happens. Any ELN with the right featu...
NIH expects funded investigators to do three things.

ResearchOS supports all three expectations. The same plan / create / implement structure other ELN vendors use to map their features to the policy applies here, with the bonus that your data lives in open formats on your own disk the entire time.

Use separate projects to model how each grant's data will be organized, and reusable methods and templates so the whole lab captures data the same way from day one. Planning the structure up front is most of what a DMS Plan's data-organization section asks you to describe.

The plan itself is a short document built against NIH's required format. Most institutions point researchers at the free DMPTool , which has the official NIH template built in. Draft it there, then keep the finished plan in ResearchOS as a note or attachment alongside the proj...

This is where the day-to-day work lives, and where ResearchOS is strongest. The table below maps what the policy and NIH's Desirable Characteristics of Data Repositories guidance expect against the ResearchOS feature that delivers it.

The DMS Plan names a repository, and that repository is where sharing happens, not the notebook. NIH does not mandate a specific one. It publishes Desirable Characteristics of Data Repositories (persistent identifiers, long-term stewardship, curation, clear reuse terms) and ex...

ResearchOS gets you to that handoff cleanly with a guided deposit dialog. You pick the repository (Zenodo, Figshare, or another of your choice), and ResearchOS prefills the deposit metadata in the DataCite shape repositories expect (title, creator with ORCID, abstract, license...

ResearchOS supports your DMS compliance, and it is honest about where a feature is a manual step rather than a built-in one. None of these block compliance, but you should know them before you write your plan.
Do not anchor a compliance decision to a vendor blog (including this one). The primary sources are short and readable.
Wondering how ResearchOS stacks up against the tool most labs are leaving? See ResearchOS vs LabArchives .
Plan and budget for managing and sharing scientific data, before the work starts.
Write a DMS Plan (a two-page document) and submit it with the funding application.
Implement the plan by managing the data rigorously during the project, then share it no later than publication or the end of the award, whichever comes first.
NIH DMS Policy overview and the policy hub at sharing.nih.gov .
NOT-OD-21-013 is the Final NIH Policy itself.
NOT-OD-21-014 lists the required elements of a DMS Plan. This is the real checklist.
NOT-OD-21-016 covers desirable characteristics of data repositories.

Not a DMS certificate. The enterprise price tag buys security certifications (FedRAMP, SOC 2, ISO 27001, 21 CFR Part 11), hosted backups, institutional administration, and brand trust. The "NIH selected LabArchives" line you may have seen refers to NIH's intramural program (NI...

This is the part most vendor pages bury. NIH explicitly says investigators are not expected to share laboratory notebooks, preliminary analyses, drafts, or communications. What you share is a curated dataset , deposited into an appropriate repository. So a notebook's real job ...

ResearchOS has a guided deposit dialog today. It prefills your DataCite-shaped metadata, bundles the data, and opens the repository's own upload page, where the repository mints the DOI. What is not shipped yet is the fully automated one-click path, where ResearchOS publishes ...

Alongside the append-only PI audit log and soft-delete Trash, ResearchOS now keeps a full per-record version history with a restore button and a 24-hour undo on notes, tasks, and projects. You can browse prior versions, see who changed what, and roll an entry back. See Version...

ResearchOS has first-class fields for your ORCID iD (on your profile) and for funder and award numbers (on each funding account), named to match the DataCite schema a DOI deposit uses. The guided deposit dialog reads them automatically and prefills the metadata for you, so you...

ResearchOS holds no FedRAMP, SOC 2, ISO 27001, or 21 CFR Part 11 attestation. Its security posture is different by design, a local-first app with no vendor database to certify (see Security ). That is the right trade-off for many labs, but if your institution requires a certif...

entries.title: Features
bodySnippets: One short page per feature in ResearchOS. Most show a screenshot, the steps for the most common action, and any non-obvious tips.

entries.title: Use any AI with your data
breadcrumbs: Use any AI with your data
headings: Flow 1: paste into a chat assistant
Flow 2: point an agent at your folder
What it can and cannot do
Why this works here and not in a cloud notebook
bodySnippets: Because your notebook is plain files on your own disk, the AI you already use can read it. ResearchOS ships a built-in AI Helper, a prompt that teaches any model how your data is structured, so it can draft entries, cross-reference protocols, and answer questions about your ow...

Open Settings and find the AI Helper section. It gives you a schema-aware prompt, a block of text that explains how a ResearchOS folder is laid out (where notes, experiments, methods, and projects live, and what each file looks like). Hand that to any model and it understands ...
You choose a size to fit the model you are using.
The simplest way. No setup, works with any chat AI, and nothing leaves your machine except the text you choose to paste.

The more powerful way. An agentic tool with read access to your data folder can work across your whole notebook at once, no copy-paste. Give it the AI Helper prompt plus access to the folder and it can draft entries, fill in notes, and cross-reference protocols alongside you.
This works with any tool that can read a local folder, for example:

An AI can read anything in the folder you give it access to, and draft content for you to place. Whether it can write files depends on the tool. A paste-flow chat AI only suggests text you copy back, while a folder-access agent can write files directly if you let it. Treat age...
Minimal (around 6k tokens) is for tiny windows or small local models.
Lean (around 21k tokens) fits most chat windows.
Full (around 42k tokens) is best for drafting on big-context models like Claude, GPT-5, or Gemini 2.5 Pro.
A coding agent like Claude Code or Cursor, opened on your data folder.
A filesystem MCP server, so an assistant can list and read your files.
A local model (via Ollama, LM Studio, and the like) pointed at the folder, so your data never leaves your machine at all.

Copy the prompt. In Settings, pick a size and click copy, or use the one-click button to open it directly in Claude, ChatGPT, Gemini, or Copilot. (Microsoft Copilot is free with many university accounts.)

Paste in your data or your question. Paste the prompt, then paste the note or experiment you want help with, or just ask. Because the prompt taught the model your schema, it knows what a running-log note or a PCR method looks like and can draft in the same shape.
Bring the result back. Copy the model's draft back into the matching field in ResearchOS. You stay in control of what gets saved.

Your projects, notes, and protocols are ordinary JSON and Markdown files in a folder you control, so any AI can read them. A cloud notebook stores your data on its servers, encrypted at rest, behind its own login, so no outside AI can reach it and you are left with whatever as...

Paste the AI Helper prompt, then: "You have read access to my ResearchOS folder. Read project 3, summarize what is left to do, and draft a running-log entry for today's qPCR run from these Cq values..." The agent reads the real files and drafts in your notebook's format.

None of this uploads your research anywhere unless you choose a cloud-hosted model and paste data into it. A local model or a local folder-access agent keeps everything on your own computer. The choice, and the data, stay yours.
The AI Helper in Settings. Pick a size, copy the prompt, or open it straight in your provider.
The AI Helper section in Settings, with size options and one-click buttons to open the prompt in Claude, ChatGPT, Gemini, or Copilot.

entries.title: Calendar
headings: A tab has to be open
bodySnippets: A month, week, and day calendar for your conferences, deadlines, and meetings, with optional overlays from Google, Outlook, and iCloud.

The Calendar page lays out your ResearchOS events on a grid alongside any read-only items pulled in from a linked Google, Outlook, or iCloud calendar. Both kinds of event render with a filled colored background. The text color flips between black and white automatically so the...

The header strip holds the current date heading, prev/next arrows, a Today shortcut, and a month / week / day toggle. The two buttons on the far right are Linked Calendars (connect Google, Outlook, or paste an iCal URL) and + New Event .

Click a ResearchOS event block to open its details popover. Edit swaps the popover into a form with every field, including a "Clear times (make all-day)" shortcut. Save writes the change. Delete removes the event after a confirmation.

Click Linked Calendars in the top right (or Manage in the sidebar) to open the modal. You can also navigate to directly, which auto-opens the modal so you can share a deep-link that drops someone straight into the add-feed flow. The same iCal-URL flow works for all four provid...

Every linked calendar gets a color in the sidebar legend. The Linked Calendars modal also has a per-feed color picker so you can recolor any feed to match your preferences without disconnecting and re-adding it. Toggle a feed on or off from either the modal or the legend. Remo...

Clicking a linked event opens a read-only popover with the title, time, location, and any notes the source calendar published. To edit a linked event, open it in its source app (Google Calendar, Outlook, Apple Calendar) and the change shows up in ResearchOS the next time it re...
Want the deep-dive? Calendar Feeds walks through per-provider setup with screenshots.

Reminders are a single global preference, set from the Reminders link at the top of the calendar sidebar. The modal lets you turn reminders on, pick how far ahead you want to be notified (anywhere from 1 minute to 1 day before), and optionally grant browser-notification permis...

When a timed event's reminder fires, ResearchOS adds an entry to the bell inbox in the top bar. If you've granted browser permission, it also raises a system notification you can click to jump back to the tab. All-day events don't produce reminders, since there's no obvious mo...
On the Calendar page the usual left sidebar swaps in a calendar-specific one with two sections.

Month is a 7-column grid of day cells. Each cell shows up to three events, and the rest collapse into a count (a non-clickable label, not a link). Single-click a day to open a day-detail drawer listing every event on that date. Double-click to start a new event prefilled with ...

Week is a 7-day stack with an all-day strip across the top and an hourly time grid below. Click any empty 15-minute slot in the grid to open the New Event popover prefilled with that date and time. A red "now" line tracks the current minute on today's column.
Day shows the same hourly grid for a single day, also with a red "now" line tracking the current minute. Useful first thing in the morning, and roomy enough to show an event's location inline.
Google Calendar exposes a per-calendar Secret address in iCal format under Settings → Settings for my calendars. Copy that URL, paste it into the form.
Outlook / Microsoft 365 publishes a calendar from Settings → Calendar → Shared calendars → Publish a calendar. Copy the ICS link Outlook generates, paste it into the form.
iCloud / Apple Calendar shares via Calendar app → right-click → Share Calendar → Public Calendar. Paste the URL as-is. ResearchOS rewrites it automatically.
Other (any iCal URL) accepts any standard feed URL, so you can add lab-managed or third-party calendars that aren't one of the three named providers.

Calendars lists ResearchOS events plus every linked feed, color-matched to the grid. Click a feed row to hide or show its events without disconnecting it. Click the color swatch on any row to recolor it, including the ResearchOS events row, so you can override the per-type def...
Upcoming shows the next 30 days, grouped by date (Today / Tomorrow / weekday + date) with up to 40 entries. Click any item to jump the main calendar to that day in day view.

Click + New Event in the top right. You can also double-click a day in month view, or single-click an empty slot in week or day view, to open the popover prefilled with that date (and a 15-minute snapped start time when applicable).
Fill in a Title , pick a Type (Conference, Deadline, Meeting, or Other), and set the start and end dates. Leaving both time fields blank makes it an all-day event.
Optionally add a Location , a URL , a Color from the palette, and free-form Notes .
Click Create Event . The block appears in the selected date(s) in whichever view is active.

The month/week/day toggle changes your view for the current session only. The change is ephemeral. When you reload the app, the calendar returns to its persisted startup view. To change that startup view, open Settings → View defaults and pick a different starting view. Resear...
If you don't pick a color, the event uses its type's default, which is purple for conferences, red for deadlines, blue for meetings, and gray for other.

The event create/edit modal includes a Mark as PTO day checkbox. Events flagged PTO display a badge on their chip in the calendar grid. ResearchOS also factors PTO days into streaks and project schedule calculations, so marking time off keeps your progress tracking accurate.
A public iCal URL grants read access to every event on that calendar to anyone who has the link. ResearchOS only stores it in your private data folder, but treat the URL itself like a password.

Reminders run in JavaScript inside an open ResearchOS tab. If every tab is closed at the moment the reminder is due, the real-time popup won't fire, but the reminder is still waiting in the bell inbox the next time you open the app.
The Calendar page in month view, with native event blocks and the +New Event button in the top right.
The Linked Calendars modal showing the Connected list at top and an iCal URL form below.

entries.title: Chemistry
bodySnippets: The Chemistry workbench is where you draw structures, build a molecule library, and find the papers and patents that mention a compound, all in the browser and all filed alongside your projects.

The Chemistry workbench gives you two tools that chemists normally pay a per-seat license for. The first is a structure editor in the style of ChemDraw, a full drawing surface for molecules that runs entirely in your browser. The second is a literature and patent search in the...

The reason it works without a backend is that the chemistry itself runs in your browser. The drawing surface, the structure cleanup, the molecular formula and weight, the canonical SMILES and InChIKey, all of it is computed locally by chemistry libraries compiled to run in the...

The left rail of the workbench is your molecule library. It lists every molecule in your folder, each row carrying a small rendered structure thumbnail, the name, and the molecular formula and average weight. This is the same signature left-rail layout the Sequences and Data H...

A collection selector at the top of the rail groups molecules by project, so you can narrow the list to one project, view the molecules not yet filed under any project, or open the library to everything at once. Each option carries a live count. Below the selector, a search bo...

Search by structure. Beyond the name filter, a search-by-structure mode finds molecules in your own library by chemistry rather than text. You draw a fragment to match every molecule that contains that substructure, or rank the library by similarity to a reference structure, s...

Right-click and bulk actions. Right-clicking a molecule in the rail opens quick actions to rename it, duplicate it, send it to a note, experiment, or method, or delete it. Selecting several rows with their checkboxes lets you delete a batch or file it all under a project at once.
There are three ways to add a molecule, surfaced as the New, PubChem, and Import actions at the top of the library rail.

Draw a new structure. The New action opens the structure editor on a blank canvas. You draw the molecule with bonds, rings, templates, and the periodic table, then save it to the library. The editor is the same surface described under Drawing and editing structures below.

Import from PubChem. The PubChem action opens a search box. Type a compound name and the search returns a grid of candidate compounds from PubChem, each with its structure, formula, and weight, so you can pick the right one rather than guessing from a name alone. Importing a c...

Import a structure file. The Import action accepts MDL Molfiles (.mol), SDF files (.sdf, where each record lands as its own molecule), and SMILES files (.smi, .smiles). Everything is parsed in your browser, so the file never leaves your machine. A single-molecule import drops ...

Clicking a molecule in the rail opens its detail view. This is the fast browse surface, and it does not load the heavy editor, so moving between molecules is instant. At the top is a large rendered depiction of the structure. Below it is an identity table with the molecular fo...

Quick copy actions put the canonical SMILES, the InChIKey, or a reference to the molecule on your clipboard, and a Send to action pushes the molecule straight into a note, experiment, or method. The molecule reference pastes into a note as a chip that links back to this molecu...

Each molecule is written to your folder as a Molfile (the source of truth) next to a small sidecar holding the name, the source, the project links, and the computed identity. The Molfile is a standard format, so your structures stay readable by ChemDraw, RDKit, Open Babel, and...
The Chemistry workbench. The molecule library sits on the left, and the selected molecule opens in the detail view on the right.

The Chemistry workbench showing the molecule library rail on the left, a list of molecules with structure thumbnails, and a selected molecule's detail view on the right with its structure depiction and identity facts.
The collection selector scopes the library to one project, to the unfiled molecules, or to everything at once.
The molecule library rail with the collection selector open, showing All molecules, Unfiled, and a list of project names each with a molecule count.
PubChem import returns a grid of candidates so you can choose the right compound, not just the first name match.
The PubChem import dialog with the word caffeine typed in the search box and a grid of candidate compounds below, each showing a rendered structure, name, formula, and molecular weight.
The detail view. Identity facts are computed locally from the structure; the copy actions put the SMILES, InChIKey, or a note reference on your clipboard.

The molecule detail view showing a large structure depiction on the left, an identity table with formula, average molecular weight, canonical SMILES, and InChIKey on the right, copy actions beneath, and a linked-projects section.

entries.title: Cloning Workspace
headings: Split out of the Sequences page
The assembled construct is validated, not estimated
bodySnippets: The Cloning Workspace assembles new constructs in silico. Pick a chemistry, add your fragments, and review the product before it ever touches the bench.

Cloning is the act of joining DNA fragments into a new molecule. Before you order primers or set up a reaction at the bench, it helps to know exactly what the assembled construct will be, where each fragment lands, and which oligos you need. The Cloning Workspace is the tool t...

The Workspace opens from the Assemble button in the header of the Sequences library, not from an editor tab or the top navigation. Inside, a single method picker offers four assembly chemistries, and every one of them runs the same two-step flow. First you pick and order your ...

Every chemistry shares the same shape, so once you have done one assembly the rest feel familiar. The first step is the pick step. You choose the method, add the fragments or substrate molecules the reaction needs (from the library or pasted directly), order and orient them, a...

Overlap assembly joins an ordered set of fragments that share short homologous ends. At the bench you amplify each fragment with primers whose 5' ends carry a homology tail matching the neighbouring fragment. The exonuclease chews back, the matching single strands anneal, and ...

In the Workspace you add two or more fragments in the order you want them joined, choose whether the product is linear or circular, decide whether to carry the source fragments' annotations onto the product (on by default), and pick how the homology overlap is sized. You can f...

The designed oligos are yours to use directly. The review step offers them as a copyable oligo order list, or you can have them saved as features on the product so the primers travel with the template they belong to. The junction report flags any overlap that came out shorter ...

Restriction-ligation is the classic cut-and-paste of molecular cloning. A restriction enzyme cuts double-stranded DNA at a defined site, and when the two strands are cut at offset positions it leaves a single-stranded overhang (a sticky end). Two ends ligate when their overhan...

In the Workspace you choose two or more sequences and a set of common cutters (EcoRI, BamHI, HindIII, PstI, KpnI, SmaI, XhoI, and NotI). The engine digests each sequence on both strands, types every resulting end as blunt, a 5' overhang, or a 3' overhang, and then enumerates t...

Golden Gate assembly is a one-pot, scarless method built on Type IIS enzymes. Unlike a classic restriction enzyme, a Type IIS enzyme such as BsaI, BsmBI, BbsI, or SapI cuts outside and downstream of its recognition site. That means the recognition sequence ends up on a flankin...

In the Workspace you add your parts and choose a Type IIS enzyme. The engine digests every part with that enzyme, drops the pieces that still carry the recognition site (the flanks), and ligates the central parts by their custom overhangs into a seamless product. The review st...

Gateway cloning moves a DNA segment between vectors by site-specific recombination, with no restriction digestion and no ligation. The reaction happens between short att sites that share a common core. BP Clonase recombines an attB-flanked insert with an attP donor to make an ...

In the Workspace you pick the reaction (BP or LR) and the two substrate molecules. The engine locates the att sites on each substrate by matching the published site sequences on both strands, checks that each substrate presents one site 1 and one site 2, and then computes each...

Whichever chemistry you used, the review step is the same surface. It shows the assembled product sequence with its features, the junctions it formed, and any warnings. Features from each input fragment are rebased into the product coordinates, so a CDS or a promoter that star...

A product saved from the Cloning Workspace is a first-class sequence in the Sequences library, filed under the active collection. It opens in the same editor, renders the same circular and linear maps, carries the features the assembly rebased onto it, and goes through the sam...

This page used to be one section of the Sequences workbench page. The cloning chemistries grew their own depth, so they now live here. The Cloning Workspace is still launched from inside the Sequences library, and the products it makes land back in that same library.

Overlap assembly takes each fragment in the orientation you supply it (top strand, 5' to 3', left to right). The engine does not silently reverse-complement a fragment to find a better junction, which keeps the result predictable. Order and orient the fragments the way the con...

Gateway substrates are normally supercoiled circles. The donor and destination vectors must be circular to recombine, and the entry clone in an LR reaction should be circular too, while the attB-PCR product fed into a BP reaction can be linear. The review step warns you when a...

A wrong cloning product is a real molecular-biology error, so the assembly engines are not approximations. The overlap and cut-and-ligate products are cross-validated against the independent pydna simulator, and the Gateway crossover is validated against the published canonica...
The Cloning Workspace. Pick a chemistry from the four method tabs, add fragments from your library or paste a sequence, then set the method's options.

The Cloning Workspace open over the Sequences library, showing the four method tabs (Overlap, Restriction, Golden Gate, Gateway) across the top, a fragments-in-order panel on the left, and the DNA library on the right.

entries.title: Cloud storage and plans
headings: Your data lives on your computer
Three ways data can leave your machine
Plans and how it is paid for
Why it can stay this cheap
During the beta, everything is free
bodySnippets: Your notebook is local-first and yours. The cloud is an optional layer for sync, sharing, and real-time collaboration, and a whole lab shares one allowance that only the lab head ever pays for.

Every note, experiment, method, sequence, image, and file is a plain file in a folder you own. ResearchOS reads and writes that folder, and that folder is always the original. Nothing uploads unless you choose to sync, share, or co-edit, so private work never leaves your machi...

The cloud does not hold your research. It holds only the optional copies used for the three paths below, which is why the storage limits are a limit on cloud copies, not on how much research you can do.
Each path is different, so it is worth being precise about what uploads and how it is protected.
The cloud copies count against a free allowance, and the key fact is that a lab shares one allowance no matter how many members join. Only the lab head pays. It is not per-person.

The local-first core is free and open source under the AGPLv3, with every feature included. It is supported by a UW Distinguished Research Fellowship at UW-Madison and by voluntary contributions. Cloud storage above the free pool comes as flat-price plans. Individuals and labs...

The plans are flat, not metered per gigabyte, so the bill is a known monthly number with no surprises. If a lab leans on the cloud heavily, the lab head can move the lab to a larger plan. Specific plan prices are still being set from real usage, so they are not published yet.

If you want to support the project beyond your own use, the best way is to buy only the storage you actually use and to support us through GitHub Sponsors. A sponsorship is a direct contribution that funds development, and a donation is not subject to sales tax the way a produ...

Reaching the cap means paused, not charged. If a lab fills its pool, cloud sync pauses and nobody is billed by surprise. The local-first app keeps working against the folder on each person's disk, and sync resumes once space is freed or the lab head moves to a larger plan. You...
Accounts and tiers covers Local-only, Free, and Lab accounts. How it stays free explains the funding model, and Sharing and permissions covers who can see what.
Private, on your disk. Your own notes and files stay on your machine. Nothing uploads unless you pick one of the two paths below.
A one-time send. Send a note, method, project, or photo to someone. It is end-to-end encrypted and transient, deleted the moment they pick it up, and it does not count against the storage pool.

Real-time collaboration. So collaborators stay in sync even when one of them is offline, the server holds the shared copy. It is encrypted in transit and at rest. This is the only copy that counts against the storage allowance.
One 5 GB free pool per lab. The whole lab shares it. Adding members does not add more free space, because the pool belongs to the lab, not to each seat.
Only the PI ever pays. Members never pay and never see a bill. They work against the lab's ceiling, so a full lab is the PI's decision, not a member's problem.
Solo users get their own 5 GB. A solo researcher has the same free tier as a lab, independent of any lab.

Notes and methods are tiny, so a lab would need roughly half a million text notes to fill the free pool. The pool only fills with the heavy things, mostly images and large file attachments in shared work. A gel or bench photo is about the size of a photo from a modern phone, a...

Because the app is local-first, your everyday work never touches our servers, so our costs are small and the price can be too. Bulk storage costs us about a penny and a half per gigabyte-month, so a 5 GB free pool costs us pennies and the paid plans only need to recover real c...
Cloud billing is not turned on yet. The whole system is free while we gather real usage to set the plan prices, so nothing charges you today.

entries.title: Companion
headings: The five things it does at the bench
bodySnippets: The Companion is a phone app that pairs to the laptop running ResearchOS, so the work you do at the bench (snap a gel, scan a handwritten page, glance at today, follow a method, track stock) lands in the right experiment without walking back to your computer. Your phone is whe...

Most lab notebooks assume you are sitting at a keyboard. Real lab work happens standing at a bench with gloves on, a gel in one hand and a timer running. The Companion exists for that moment. It is a small, focused app for iOS and Android that talks to your laptop, so a photo ...

The Companion is deliberately small. It does the handful of things that are genuinely better on a phone than on a laptop, and it sends each result back to the laptop where the rest of your notebook lives.

The Companion ships as a dev-client build for iOS and Android, not as a listing you install from Expo Go. Once you have the app on your phone, the only setup is a one-time pairing to a laptop running ResearchOS, covered on the Pairing page. There is no account to create and no...

If you want to see what the app does before pairing a real laptop, the home screen offers a Try the demo path. It runs the app against sample data with a fake pairing, so you can walk through capture, scanning, today, methods, and inventory with no laptop in the loop.

The Companion talks to the laptop through a relay server, but that relay is built so it can never read what passes through it. Every snapshot the laptop sends and every command the phone sends is sealed end-to-end to the phone's own X25519 key with authenticated encryption, so...

Captured images go one step further. The moment the laptop pulls a photo off the relay, that photo is deleted from the relay. The relay is a hand-off point, not storage. And like the rest of ResearchOS, the Companion is local-first, so your captures, notes, and timers stay on ...
New to the Companion? Pair your phone first, then work through the actions in whatever order matches your bench.
Capture and route a photo. Take or upload a photo, caption it, and file it straight into an experiment's Lab Notes or Results. See Capture and route .
Scan a handwritten page. Scan a notebook page and the handwriting becomes searchable text on the laptop, read on the device. See Scanning handwritten notes .
Glance at today. Pull down a list of what is scheduled, overdue, and coming up. See Today glance .
Read a method. Open the method for the experiment you are running, big and scrollable, and log a variation from the bench. See View a method on your phone .
Track inventory. Barcode-scan to count stock down, see what is low, and mark a purchase as arrived. See Inventory scanning .
A dev-client build of the Companion on iOS or Android.
A one-time pairing to a laptop running ResearchOS before first use.
Camera permission, used for the pairing QR scan and for photos.
Notification permission is optional. Timers run without it; you just will not get a banner when one finishes.
Pairing , the one-time QR setup that links your phone to a laptop.
Capture and route , photos that file themselves into the right experiment.
Scanning handwritten notes , handwriting that becomes searchable text.
Today glance , the pull-down of what is due.
View a method on your phone , read steps and log a variation at the bench.
Inventory scanning , barcode-scan to track stock.

A dev-client build of the Companion on iOS or Android. A one-time pairing to a laptop running ResearchOS before first use. Camera permission, used for the pairing QR scan and for photos. Notification permission is optional. Timers run without it; you just will not get a banner...

entries.title: Capture and route
headings: How routing reaches the right experiment
No experiment focused? It lands in your Inbox
Local-first means you can shoot anywhere
bodySnippets: Take a photo at the bench, caption it, and send it straight into the experiment it belongs to. Capture and route is the difference between a phone full of unlabeled gel photos and a notebook where every image is already filed under the right experiment, in the right tab, the m...

The everyday problem the Companion solves is the camera roll. You photograph a gel, a plate, a colony, a whiteboard, and three days later you are squinting at twenty near-identical images trying to remember which experiment each one was for. Capture and route fixes that at the...
A capture is three small decisions made in a row, all on the phone, all at the bench.

Capture. Take a new photo with the camera, or upload one you already have on the phone. The image queues locally first, so you can shoot even with no signal and it uploads once the phone is paired and online.

Caption. Add a short caption while you still remember what you are looking at ("Colony PCR, lanes 1 to 8, expecting 1.2 kb"). This is the line you will be glad you wrote when you find the image later.
Route. Pick the destination in the Notebook Chooser sheet. You choose the experiment and whether the image lands in its Lab Notes or its Results tab.

When you pick a destination, the phone sends the laptop a sealed route-capture command alongside the image. That command tells the laptop which experiment and which tab to file the photo into, so the laptop does the filing rather than you doing it by hand later. The command is...

Some images are worth marking up, circling the product band, arrowing the colony you picked. The Companion lets you draw an annotation overlay on a capture, and that overlay is saved as a separate file next to the image rather than painted into the photo. The original capture ...

If no experiment is open and focused on the laptop, a routed capture has nowhere obvious to go, so it lands in your Inbox instead of being lost. You file it into an experiment from there when you are back at the laptop, so a capture is never dropped just because nothing was se...

Captures queue on the phone and upload when paired, so a cold room or a basement scope with no signal does not stop you. Shoot the photo, caption it, choose where it goes, and the routing completes the moment the phone and laptop can reach the relay again.
A fresh capture with its caption and the destination it will be routed into.
The Companion capture screen on a phone, showing a just-taken gel photo with a caption field and a destination chooser below it.

entries.title: Inventory scanning
headings: It is the same inventory, just in your hand
Close the loop without a round trip
bodySnippets: Scan a barcode at the freezer to count stock down, see what is running low, and mark an ordered purchase as arrived. The Companion turns the phone into the scanner your inventory always wanted, so keeping stock current is a quick scan at the bench instead of a chore you do bac...

Inventory only stays accurate if updating it is nearly free. The moment it takes a walk back to the laptop and a few clicks, people stop, and the list drifts into fiction. The Companion removes that friction. The barcode is already on the tube, the phone is already in your poc...

The inventory the phone shows is delivered by the laptop as a sealed inventory snapshot, so the relay carries your stock list without being able to read it. The phone unseals the snapshot with its own key, you act on it at the bench, and your scans flow back to keep the laptop...
Scan to track stock. Scan a container barcode to count that stock down, the same one-tap finish event you would do on the laptop, made faster with the phone's camera.
See what is low. The screen shows the items running low, so a freezer check turns into a reorder list without opening your computer.
Mark a purchase arrived. When a box shows up, mark the ordered purchase as arrived from the phone, so the delivery is logged at the receiving bench where it happens.

Counting a container down on the phone is the same event as finishing one on the laptop, so the two stay in step. The full model, counting containers rather than volumes, the low and expiry signals, and how a received purchase becomes stock, is covered on the Inventory page. T...

Scan the last vial down at the freezer, see the item flip to low, and it is queued to reorder, all before you have walked back to your desk. The receiving end works the same way. A delivered box is marked arrived on the phone and becomes stock on the laptop, so ordering and in...
Scan a barcode to count a container down; low-stock items are listed below.
The Companion inventory screen on a phone, with the camera framing a reagent barcode and a list of low-stock items below.

entries.title: Pairing
headings: Why a QR code, and why no password
Try the demo without a laptop
Camera permission is for the QR and for photos
bodySnippets: Pairing is the one-time step that links your phone to a laptop running ResearchOS. You scan a QR code shown on the laptop, the two devices exchange public keys, and from then on your phone can send captures and pull glances from that laptop. There is no account and no password...

Before the Companion can do anything useful, it needs to know which laptop it is working with. A lab has many people and many machines, and a photo you snap has to land in your notebook, not someone else's. So the first thing the app asks you to do is pair, and pairing is buil...

Pairing has to do two jobs at once. It has to be quick enough that nobody avoids it, and it has to be trustworthy enough that a capture cannot be misrouted or read by anyone else. A QR code scanned in person does both. The laptop shows a code that carries a signed grant, the p...

When you scan, the phone verifies the grant is genuinely signed, then generates its own device keys and registers with the relay under those keys. From that point on, everything the laptop sends to your phone is sealed to your phone's key, so the relay in the middle only ever ...
On the laptop, open the Companion hub from the button in the app header. It shows a pairing QR code.
On the phone, open the Companion and choose to pair. Grant camera permission when asked, since the app needs the camera to read the code.
Point the phone at the QR code on the laptop screen. The phone verifies the signed grant, generates its device keys, and registers with the relay.
The app confirms the link and drops you on the home screen. You are paired, and you only do this once for that laptop.

If you want to look around before you pair anything real, the home screen has a Try the demo option. It runs a fake pairing against sample data, so the app behaves as though it is connected to a laptop full of experiments without one being present. Every screen works, nothing ...

The camera permission you grant during pairing is the same one the app uses later to take photos and scan barcodes. You are asked once, at the moment the code needs scanning, so the request makes sense in context.

Pairing is a setup step, not a daily one. After your phone is linked to your laptop, capturing a photo, scanning a page, or glancing at today just works. The next stop is Capture and route , where that first photo files itself into an experiment.
The laptop's Companion hub shows the pairing QR; the phone scans it to link the two devices.
The Companion hub on a laptop showing a pairing QR code, next to the phone's camera view framing that code during a scan.

entries.title: Scanning handwritten notes
headings: Recognition runs on your phone, not a server
The Scan button hides where there is no scanner
bodySnippets: Scan a page from a paper notebook and the handwriting becomes searchable text on your laptop. The Companion reads the page with the phone's own on-device text recognition, so the words you wrote by hand end up findable in search without you retyping a line, and without the pag...

Plenty of real lab notes still start on paper, a quick calculation, a plate map, a scribbled observation while the timer runs. Those pages are useful only if you can find them again, and a photo of handwriting is not searchable. Scanning closes that gap. You scan the page, the...

Tap Scan and hold the phone over the page. The native document scanner detects the page edges, rectifies the perspective, and cleans the image, so a photo taken at an angle comes out flat and legible.

The phone runs on-device text recognition on the cleaned scan to extract the words. On iOS this is Apple Vision; on Android it is ML Kit. Either way the recognition happens on the phone, not on a server.
Route the scan to an experiment the same way you route a photo. The laptop receives the image and the recognized text together.

When the laptop receives a scan, it stores the image and writes the recognized text into a small companion file next to it, an sidecar. The instruction to write that sidecar arrives as a sealed ocr-sidecar command, so the relay carries it without being able to read it. Because...

Searchable handwriting connects the paper half of your work to the digital half. Once a scanned page is indexed, it shows up in search right alongside your typed notes, so where a note started, on paper or on the keyboard, stops mattering for finding it again.

The handwriting is read on the device by the phone's built-in text recognition, so the page itself is never sent off to a cloud service to be read. The relay only ever carries the sealed image and the sealed sidecar text, and it cannot open either. Your paper notes stay as pri...

Scanning leans on the operating system's native document scanner. On a device or platform where that scanner is not available, the Scan button is not shown rather than offering a worse fallback. If you do not see it, your phone does not provide the native scanner the feature r...
The native document scanner detects the page edges and cleans the scan before recognition runs.
The Companion document scanner on a phone, with a handwritten notebook page detected and its edges auto-cropped, ready to capture.

entries.title: Today glance
headings: Where it lives and how to turn it off
bodySnippets: Pull down on the Companion to see what is on for today. It lists the tasks scheduled now, anything overdue, and what is coming up. It is a quick read of your day pulled from the laptop, so you can check whether you have a culture to split or a timer to start without opening yo...

At the bench you rarely need your whole project plan. You need to know the next thing, whether something slipped, and what is around the corner. The today glance gives you exactly that and nothing more. It is a short, scannable list you reach with a pull-down, kept current by ...
The glance is organized the way a working day actually breaks down, so the thing demanding attention is at the top and the rest is context.

The phone does not compute your schedule. The laptop does, then publishes a sealed today snapshot, a small bundle of today's tasks plus the overdue and upcoming items. The phone pulls that snapshot and unseals it with its own key. Because the snapshot is sealed end-to-end, the...

The today glance is the read-only counterpart to planning you do on the laptop. The schedule itself is built and edited there, on the calendar and across your projects, and the glance is simply that plan, delivered to your hand at the bench.
Overdue. Tasks whose date has already passed, surfaced first so nothing quietly slips.
Today. What is scheduled for the current day, the core of the list.
Upcoming. What is on the near horizon, so you can see what is coming before it arrives.

The glance is a collapsible pull-down on the Notebook tab, and it is on by default because most people want the quick read. If you would rather not see it, you can toggle it off in Settings, and the Notebook tab goes back to a clean start.
The today glance pulled down on the Notebook tab, grouped into overdue, today, and upcoming.
The Companion Notebook tab on a phone with the today glance pulled down, listing scheduled tasks, an overdue item, and upcoming items in three groups.

entries.title: View a method on your phone
breadcrumbs: View a method on your phone
headings: Send a method to the phone
Log a variation from the bench
Why the projection is sealed and read-only
The method and your reality stay in sync
bodySnippets: Send the method for the experiment you are running to your phone, where it reads big and scrolls cleanly at the bench. When you change something on the fly, log the variation right there and it lands back on the laptop as a timestamped note on that method, so what you actually...

Following a protocol at the bench off a laptop screen is awkward. The screen is across the room, the text is small, and your hands are busy. Viewing the method on your phone puts the steps where you are working, sized to read at arm's length. And because real protocols drift, ...
On the laptop, open the experiment and go to its Method tab.
Press View method on phone . This button appears when a phone is paired, so it shows up exactly when it can do something.
The laptop publishes a sealed, read-only projection of the method, and the phone renders it large and scrollable for the bench.

The phone renders whichever kind of method the experiment uses, so a structured protocol stays structured rather than collapsing into a wall of text. That covers a PCR program with its thermal steps, an LC-MS method, a compound method, and a plain Markdown protocol. The projec...

When you deviate from the written method, tap Add a variation and describe what you actually did. The phone posts that back to the laptop, where it lands as a timestamped variation note on that experiment's method. You capture the real conditions in the moment they happen, ins...

The method is sent as a sealed projection, so the relay passes it to your phone without being able to read your protocol. It is read-only because the method of record lives on the laptop, where version history tracks every change. The phone is the bench-side reading surface, a...

The written protocol is the plan; the variation note is what occurred. Keeping both, with the variation timestamped against the method, is what makes the record reproducible. Pair this with a routed photo of the result and the experiment carries both what you intended and what...
A method projected to the phone, rendered large and scrollable, with the option to log a variation.
A method rendered on a phone in the Companion, with large scrollable steps and an Add a variation button at the bottom.

entries.title: Data Hub
headings: What Data Hub is and why it exists
The data grid and live summary
Referencing Data Hub in notes and results
Connection to the rest of the app
bodySnippets: Data Hub is a free, open-source GraphPad Prism alternative built into ResearchOS. It runs your statistics and your publication figures entirely in the browser, so your data never leaves your folder.

Prism and its peers are graphical front ends over math that is already settled and already available as proven open-source code. The value they sell is the workflow, not the statistics. Data Hub reproduces that workflow on top of battle-tested open-source libraries, so you get...

Everything runs on your own machine, in the browser. There is no server doing the computation, so your raw numbers stay in your data folder the same way your notes and sequences do. The reason this matters is simple. Your data is yours, the math is open, and you can read the e...

Data Hub lives at and is organized like the rest of ResearchOS. Documents are scoped to projects (collections) with optional subfolders, and they stand on their own rather than being born attached to a single experiment, so one workbook can pull together data from wherever it ...

The left rail is the navigator. A collection selector at the top scopes the workbook list to one project or opens it to everything at once. Below it, the Data Tables section lists your tables in a foldered tree. Two more sections, Results and Graphs, hold the analyses and figu...
Like Prism, Data Hub shapes the table to the analysis you intend, so the grid and the available tests match how your data is actually laid out. New table offers four archetypes.

A Column table is the starting point. Each column is a treatment group and each row is a replicate. The footer shows the mean, SD, SEM, and n for every group, recomputed live as you type, with no separate summarize step.

An XY table pairs an X column with one or more Y columns, one observation per row. This is the shape for dose-response curves, time courses, and standard curves, and it unlocks correlation, linear regression, and fitted curves.

A Grouped table records two factors at once. Each row is a level of the row factor, each column group is a level of the column factor, and replicate subcolumns sit under each group. That replication is what lets a two-way ANOVA estimate the interaction between the two factors.

A Survival table records time-to-event data. Each row is a subject with a follow-up time, an event indicator (1 if the event happened, 0 if the subject was censored), and an optional group label so arms can be compared with a Kaplan-Meier curve and the log-rank test.

The grid is where you enter raw replicates. You only ever enter the numbers once. The summary statistics, every analysis, and every figure all read from the same cells, so an edit to a single replicate flows through to the footer, the results sheet, and any graph immediately. ...

New analysis offers only the tests that are valid for the open table, so you never pick a test the data cannot support. A Column table offers unpaired and paired t-tests and one-way ANOVA with Tukey comparisons, plus their rank-based counterparts (Mann-Whitney U, Wilcoxon sign...

Every result leads with a plain-language verdict, a sentence that states the practical takeaway before the numbers. Does it differ, which way, by how much. The full statistics table follows, laid out the way a methods section reads it.

Every analysis carries a Show the code toggle that reveals the exact open-source Python (scipy, statsmodels, lifelines) that reproduces it, with your real group names and values baked in. Paste it into a notebook and you get the same numbers. This is the answer to the question...

Most bench scientists are not statisticians, and the most common analysis mistake is running a t-test or an ANOVA on data that breaks the test's assumptions. The guided wizard exists to prevent that. It asks a few plain questions about what you are comparing, then it does not ...

The assumption Report Card checks normality (Shapiro-Wilk, per group) and equal variance (Brown-Forsythe across groups), reports each as a plain-language pass or fail with a one-line reason, and when an assumption fails it falls back to the matching rank-based test that does n...

New graph generates a figure from the open table. The figure is real SVG, so it stays an infinitely scalable vector for a paper and also exports as a crisp hi-DPI PNG for a slide, or copies straight to the clipboard to paste into a document.

A Column table makes a column scatter (every replicate as a point over the group mean, the default, because individual points show the real spread a reviewer wants to see) or a bar with SD or SEM error bars. An XY table makes a scatter with a fitted curve laid over it (a least...

Error bars come straight from the raw replicates, the same numbers the grid footer shows, so a figure of a table is always consistent with that table. Significance brackets are pulled from a stored ANOVA, so the right stars drop onto the figure with one toggle rather than bein...

You do not have to retype data you already have. Data Hub imports CSV files, pasted Excel ranges (with a transpose option for the common wrong-orientation case), and binary .xlsx workbooks. It detects the header row and the per-column types and maps the data onto a table. The ...

A table, an analysis, or a figure can be referenced from a note or a result. The Copy reference button writes a link that renders as a live chip wherever you paste it, and clicking the chip opens the table in Data Hub. A figure can also be dropped into a note as an image with ...

Data Hub documents participate in the same project and folder structure as notes, experiments, methods, and sequences, and they go through the same Trash flow with the same recovery window when deleted. The point of building the analysis surface into ResearchOS rather than lea...

Every Data Hub document is stored as a cell-level version-controlled document, the same technology behind Version History for notes. The dataset and the analysis choices are both versioned, so a change is never silently lost and two people editing different cells converge clea...

The statistics engine is checked against external reference values (scipy, R, and NIST) in an automated test suite, so the numbers Data Hub reports match the tools the field already trusts. The public Transparency of tests page documents this approach for the bioinformatics to...

Because the footer stats, the analysis, and the figure all read the same cells, you cannot end up with a bar chart that disagrees with the table it came from. Change a replicate and the points move, the error bars resize, and the stored test re-runs.
Data Hub. The navigator rail holds your tables, analyses, and figures; the main panel shows the data grid, the results sheet, or the figure you are working on.
The Data Hub tab with the navigator rail on the left listing data tables, analyses, and graphs, and a publication figure with significance brackets in the main panel.
New table. Pick the archetype that matches your data; the grid and the available analyses follow from it.
The New data table dialog showing the four table type tiles: Column, XY, Grouped, and Survival, each with a short description.
The results sheet. The plain-language verdict comes first, then the full statistics table, then the pairwise comparisons.
A results sheet showing a plain-language verdict at the top, an ANOVA table with SS, df, MS, F, and p columns, and a Tukey comparisons table with significance asterisks.
The guided wizard. It recommends a test and shows the assumption Report Card, switching to a rank-based test automatically when an assumption fails.
The guided analysis wizard showing a recommended test at the top and an assumption Report Card below with PASS, FAIL, and NOTE rows in plain language.
The graph editor. The figure is live SVG; every control on the right redraws it, and the export stays a true vector.

The graph editor with a column scatter figure on the left showing points, a mean line, error bars, and significance brackets, and a styling panel on the right with error-bar, color, and axis controls.
Import. Paste from Excel or pick a CSV or .xlsx file; Data Hub detects the structure and previews the table before creating it.
The import dialog showing a pasted Excel range with detected columns, a transpose toggle, and a preview of how the data will map onto a table.

entries.title: The Workbench
headings: Four tabs, four ways to slice the work
Opening an experiment, the four-tab popup
Attach a method (or a PCR protocol)
Variation Notes and per-run PCR tweaks
Projects and Notes skip the filter strip
Each column carries its own count
When to use Notes vs Lab Notes
Lists holds list tasks, not experiments
Save the run, or grow a new protocol
Pick the format by where the file is going
Exporting more than one at a time
Sub-tasks live on list tasks, not experiments
bodySnippets: The Workbench is where active research lives day to day. One page with four core tabs (Projects, Experiments, Notes, Lists), each with its own way of grouping work, plus a fifth tab that appears only for people in a 1:1. The URL is /workbench, and /experiments still redirects ...

The Workbench packs four different views of your in-flight work into one page. The tab strip across the top names them and gives each a color identity. Projects (indigo), Experiments (blue), Notes (emerald), Lists (violet). The active tab fills with its color, and the others r...

Each tab uses a different organizing principle. The Projects tab is a browsable grid of project cards. The Experiments tab arranges protocols by where they sit in the run cycle on a kanban board (ready, blocked, running, awaiting writeup), with a results zone below it. The Lis...

A fifth tab appears for some people. Lab heads, and any member who is in a 1:1, also see a role-relative Mentoring or Check-ins tab (rose), the shared advising surface for a lab-head and member pair. It is gated, so a solo user with no 1:1s never sees it.

On the Experiments and Lists tabs, a row of colored pills sits below the tab strip, one per project, plus an All projects pill on the left. Click a pill to narrow the view to that project, and click it again (or pick a different one) to clear. The filter carries across those t...

Some tasks never get filed under a project. A one-off experiment you spin up in the "Miscellaneous" standalone slot stays project-less on purpose. To move a task into or out of a project later, open it and use the project dropdown on the popup's Details tab. Alongside your rea...

The Experiments tab arranges every experiment task in your library by stage of the protocol. The in-flight work sits on a kanban board of four columns across the top, and the finished work sits in two results zones below it. The four board columns run side by side so you can s...
Experiments scheduled to begin today or recently, with no incomplete upstream task blocking the start. These are the cards you can pick up and run today, so the column reads first on the left.

Experiments that depend on an upstream task that hasn't finished yet. The blocking parent's name renders directly under the card in a small amber strip so you know what you're waiting on. Click the parent name to jump straight to that task and unblock the chain.

Experiments where today falls between the start date and the end date. Each card carries a Day N of M freshness label, and (if the experiment is part of a chain) a gray Next: pointer to the experiment that follows it so you can step through a multi-day protocol without leaving...

Experiments marked complete but with no results body or attached images on disk yet. This column is your reminder pile, the bench work is done but the writeup is still owed. When the column is empty it shows a bordered emerald chip reading "All recent experiments have results ...

Below the board, the Recent results zone holds completed experiments with results landed in the last 30 days, in a wide full-card grid. When you have results across two or more projects, the zone sub-groups them by project (a small colored dot per project header) so a busy mon...

The archive at the bottom, completed experiments older than 30 days. The zone header carries a Flat / By project toggle so you can flip between a single time-ordered grid and one block per project. There's no time cap, so the whole completion history is reachable from one plac...

Each card carries the experiment name, a project pill, the project color along the edge, a hero image (if the writeup has one), a method chip strip, and a freshness label keyed to the column it sits in. When an experiment is part of a dependency chain, the Running card shows a...
The blue button at the top right of the tab opens the New Task modal with the type pre-selected to experiment . It is the same modal you'd get from the Gantt.

The Notes tab holds every note that isn't tied to a specific experiment. Meeting notes from a Tuesday lab meeting, a running log of equipment-room weirdness, half-formed ideas you want to find again next month, they all land here, in one flat list with no section headers. The ...

The header carries a search box on the left and three type filter buttons in the middle ( All , Single , Running Logs ). Single notes are one-page records of a moment (a meeting, an idea). Running logs are timestamped multi-entry pages you append to over time (an equipment jou...

The Lists tab is the queue view of every list task you own. Lists are the non-experiment task type, things like checklists, paperwork, ordering tasks, errands, anything that isn't a protocol. The Lists tab sorts them into five stages by how their dates relate to today, stacked...

Each card carries the task name, a project color dot with the project name, and a date signal phrased relative to today ("3d overdue", "Started yesterday", "Starts in 4d"). Clicking a card expands it inline as an accordion panel. Opening one card collapses the previously-open ...
Clicking a list card expands it in place. A violet border highlights the open card, and a panel slides down below the header row. Inside the panel, you get these controls.

Clicking any experiment card opens a centered modal. The popup always opens on the Details tab, regardless of whether the experiment is ready, running, awaiting writeup, or completed. You land on the same view every time, then pick the tab that fits the task at hand.
The popup carries four tabs across the header.

The header row also holds a green completion checkmark, an Export icon (down arrow, experiments only), a Share button, a fullscreen toggle, and Delete. Drag any file onto the popup and it lands in the last-viewed editor tab's or folder, with a confirmation toast at the drop po...

The two markdown tabs hold different kinds of writing on the same experiment. Lab Notes is the during-the-run record, holding running observations, what reagents you grabbed, what went sideways, the photo you took at the bench five minutes ago. Results is the final-output writ...

Both tabs use the same editor surface, a single live markdown body with an Edit / Preview toggle, an attachment strip (Images / Files tabs) pinned below the editor, and an Add File toolbar button. Saving is explicit, so each Save checkpoint records a revertible version you can...

Drag images straight into the body to place them inline, or drop them on the strip to attach without inlining. Switch the strip to its Files tab for non-image attachments (PDFs especially). Click a file link in the prose to open or download it.

Each attached method has a collapsible amber Variation Notes panel at the top of its tab. Click + Add Note and the editor prepends a fresh heading so each entry is timestamped. Hover any rendered entry to reveal an in-place delete button. The notes save back to the experiment,...

For PCR-typed methods, the gradient editor and recipe table below the variation panel are editable per-experiment. Reset to Method drops your overrides back to the protocol's defaults, and Save Changes writes them to this task only.

Sometimes a run drifts from the protocol in a way worth recording on its own, not just a tweak but a real deviation. When a task carries a deviation note it renders in an amber box on the experiment's Details tab, so the next person to read the experiment sees exactly what was...
The Note Deviations dialog is where you write one. You describe what changed during the run, then choose what to do with it.

Click the down-arrow icon in the popup header (tooltip: Export experiment ) to open the Export dialog. The dialog heading reads Export <experiment name> and lists three format cards, each with a short description of what you get and who it's for. Click a card and the file down...

While the file builds, the dialog shows a Preparing export… spinner and disables every button (Escape and the backdrop are also locked) so you can't kick off a second export by accident. If anything goes wrong, an alert shows the error and the dialog stays open for a retry.
Overdue : list tasks whose end date has passed and that aren't complete yet. The first thing in your queue every morning.
Doing : list tasks where today falls between start and end.

Upcoming : list tasks scheduled to start within the next 14 days. Tasks starting further out are omitted from the main list, and a small gray footnote below the section reads "+ N scheduled later than 14d out" so you know they exist without cluttering the view.
Recently done : completed in the last 30 days.
Earlier : completed more than 30 days ago. Collapsed by default, so click the header to expand.
The name row lets you click the name to rename it inline. Press Enter or click away to save.
The sub-task checklist lets you tick individual items to check them off. Click an item's text to rename it, and the delete icon appears on hover to remove an item.
Add item appends a new item to the checklist when you type in the input and press Enter (or click Add ).
The Mark list complete button in the footer marks the whole list done and cascades all un-checked items to complete. Click again to mark it incomplete.
The Open full view link in the footer opens the legacy full-screen popup for the same task, useful for the Details and date-editing fields not exposed in the inline panel.
Details is the entry tab. It holds the name, dates, duration, project, tags, and the high-level toggles. If the task carries a (legacy) deviation log string, it renders here in an amber box.
Lab Notes is the during-the-run markdown body, with a bottom attachment strip (Images / Files tabs) for photos, PDFs, and other files.
Method shows one tab per attached method, with the Variation Notes panel and (for PCR methods) an editable gradient and recipe.
Results is the second markdown editor, for the final writeup (the gel image you'd put in a paper, the plot for a thesis chapter), with its own attachment strip.
Save to task results only. The deviation is recorded in this task's log and the underlying method stays untouched. Use this for a one-off change that only mattered for this run.

Fork as new method. The deviation becomes the seed of a brand-new method file, created as a child of the original with the deviation baked into its text. Use this when the change is good enough to keep as its own protocol going forward.

PDF report : a professional, printable PDF with a title page, a clickable table of contents, an outline pane (bookmarks) for jumping between sections, and inline images. Lab Notes, Results, every attached method (rendered, with per-run PCR tweaks), Sub-tasks, the Deviation log...
