Each saved link is a card with a preview image (or a solid colored block if no image), the link title, a short description, and the hostname. When a link has a category, the card shows it as a small badge in the top-left corner of the preview. Cards are grouped into category s...

Hover a card. Two small icons appear in the top-right corner of the preview image, a pencil to edit (reopens the same form pre-filled), and a trash can to delete (prompts for confirmation). The hostname line at the bottom of each card is auto-derived from the URL. You only eve...

When someone sets a link to Whole lab , it shows up on your Links page too, carrying their avatar and a Shared by label so you can tell it apart from your own. The pencil and trash icons stay hidden on a shared-in card, only the person who owns it can edit or remove it, or swi...

Per the on-disk JSON, a link is a title, a URL, an optional description, an optional category, a color, and an optional preview image URL, plus who owns it and whether it's shared with the whole lab. There's no labmate ordering. Links you keep private stay in your own folder, ...
Click Add Link in the top right of the page. A form expands inline below the button.

Paste a URL into the URL field. Click the small image-icon button next to it and, if the title is still blank, it seeds the title with the site's hostname (for example addgene.org ). It does this in your browser, with no server call, so nothing is sent off and no description o...

Give the link a Title (required), pick or type a Category (the dropdown suggests Protocol, Database, Tool, Reference, Supplier, Publication, Software, Other, but any text works), and pick one of the eight color swatches.

Set Visibility . Just me (the default) keeps the link private to you. Whole lab puts it on every lab member's Links page, where they can see and open it. You stay the owner, so only you can edit or remove it.
Optionally add a Description and override the Preview Image URL with a custom image.
Click Create Link . The new card appears in its category.
The Links tab only appears if you answered "Yes" to the setup wizard question about storing bookmarks (Q7). If you don't see the tab, open Settings and re-run the setup wizard to enable it.
The color swatch only appears when no preview image is set. If you supply both a color and a preview image URL, the image takes precedence and the color is not shown.

Links sit in your own folder under , so each labmate keeps their own wall. A new link is private to you. If you want, you can flip a single link to Whole lab so everyone in the lab sees it on their own page. There's no all-or-nothing switch, you choose this link by link.
Link cards grouped by category. Each card opens its URL in a new tab when clicked.
The Links page showing link cards grouped into category sections, each card with a preview image and a title underneath.

entries.title: The Markdown Editor
headings: One surface, type markdown and watch it render
What a real ResearchOS note looks like
No metadata popup on file tiles
bodySnippets: Wherever you write prose in ResearchOS (experiment notes, task descriptions, results write-ups, methods bodies, free-form notes), you're using the same markdown editor. It's one live writing surface where you type plain markdown and watch it render as you go. Learning its tool...
The same editor opens in every place you write more than a sentence in ResearchOS.

Everything you type is plain markdown. The toolbar buttons just insert the same characters you'd type by hand (e.g., , ), so opening any of these files in a normal text editor outside ResearchOS gets you the same content.

There's nothing to configure and no block to click into. The editor is a single, continuous writing surface, like a normal document. You type plain markdown and the editor renders it live around your cursor. Headings look like headings, bold looks bold, and images show as imag...

The key is what happens at your cursor. Markdown markers (the around bold, the on a heading, a link target) stay hidden while you read, so the line looks finished. Move your caret onto that line and the markers reveal themselves, ready to edit; move away and they tuck back beh...
Every editor has a single toolbar along the top. From left to right it carries these controls.
Use Cmd on macOS and Ctrl on Windows and Linux anywhere this table says Cmd .

Images and files live in one place, a single attachment strip along the bottom of the editor, toggled with the toolbar's Attachments button. A small Images / Files tab bar above the strip switches it between image thumbnails and file tiles. There's no separate Files panel and ...
There are four ways to get a new image in.

Every image lands in an folder adjacent to the document on disk (e.g., ), and the markdown body references it with . Filenames with spaces render inline just fine. The reference looks like and the editor resolves it to the right file without any extra escaping.

The Images tab in the bottom strip lists every image in the document's folder as a row of thumbnails, whether or not the body references the image yet. Images already referenced look normal. Images that exist on disk but aren't referenced yet (e.g., a fresh arrival from the co...
Click any rendered image and a size popover appears inline with 25% , 50% , 75% , and 100% options. The choice writes a width attribute into the markdown so the size persists.

If an image reference points at a file that no longer exists (e.g., you renamed the file outside the app, or the relink hasn't synced from a teammate yet), a small red "Image Not Found" popup appears in the bottom-right corner of the editor. It lists similar-named files in the...

Anything that isn't an image (PDFs, CSVs, sequence files, protocols, archives) drops into a sibling folder and shows up as a clickable hyperlink in the prose, not as an inline preview. Switch the bottom strip to its Files tab to manage them. The add flow mirrors images, so you...
Click any link in Edit or Preview mode and a small View / Download popup appears centered on screen.

When a link points at a file that isn't in the document's folder, the same red corner popup that handles broken images opens with a File Not Found heading. There's no similar-name search for files (the recovery is usually to remove the dead link), so the popup goes straight to...
A typical note in a working lab is mostly tables, measurements, and small annotations. The four examples below are the shape to aim for.
Standard GitHub-flavored markdown all works.
The editor includes a collapsible helper panel on the left side with two tabs at the top, Shortcuts and Style Guide . Click the arrow in the panel header to collapse or expand it.

The editor doesn't autosave. You save explicitly, and every save is a checkpoint , a permanent, revertible version of the document. Click Save checkpoint in the toolbar (or press Cmd + S ) to write your edits to disk and record a version you can come back to.

Next to the save button, the Version history button opens a docked sidebar listing every checkpoint, newest first, grouped by day. Select a version and the editor body flips to a read-only diff view showing exactly what changed between that checkpoint and the one before it (ad...

If you have write access, the sidebar footer offers a Restore action. Restoring writes the chosen version back as the current content and records it as a fresh checkpoint, so the restore is itself revertible. You can always roll forward again to the pre-restore state. The time...

On save, the editor also runs a quick cleanup pass over the document's and folders: anything sitting on disk that nothing in the markdown points at gets deleted, so deleted snippets don't leave dangling files behind. The sweep matches links the way the body writes them, so URL...
See Version History for the full picture of how checkpoints, diffs, and restore work across ResearchOS.
The Lab Notes tab of any experiment popup (see Experiments & Notes ).
The Description field on the task detail popup, which you can open from the Gantt , the left sidebar, search results, or the calendar.
The Results tab inside the Workbench project view (the old standalone Results page was retired; now redirects to Workbench ).
The body of every method in the Methods library .
Free-form notes (running logs and standalone notes).
The Edit | Preview toggle (covered above).
Focus mode . The expand glyph drops the editor into a full-screen distraction-free writing view (also Cmd + Shift + F ).
Add Image (or Add File on surfaces that accept any file type) to pick attachments from disk.
Browse to insert an image already attached to this record without re-uploading.
Attachments to show or hide the bottom attachment strip (see Attachments ).

On surfaces that own their own save (the experiment popup's Lab Notes / Results tabs, the task popup), the Version history button and the Save checkpoint button ride at the right end of this same bar (see Saving ).
Click the toolbar's Add Image button to pick one or more files from disk.

Drag image files into the editor body . While you're dragging from Finder, a blue ring lights up around the popup or editor card so you know the drop will be caught. Release inside text and the image inserts at that position. Release outside text and it appends to the bottom o...
Paste from the clipboard . Copy a screenshot (e.g., macOS Cmd + Shift + 4 , then Cmd + V in the editor) and it lands inline.
Click Browse to pick from images already attached to this record (the gallery picker). Useful when the image is already on disk and you just want to insert a reference without re-uploading.

Click a thumbnail to open the image metadata popup. The popup shows a larger preview and lets you edit the caption, rename the file, delete it from disk, or jump to where it's used in the body. If the image isn't referenced anywhere yet, the jump button is disabled. The popup ...
Drag a thumbnail into the editor to insert that image at the cursor position. The thumbnail stays in the strip so the same image can appear twice in the document.

Drag a thumbnail to the trash zone at the bottom of the editor. ResearchOS deletes the file from disk and removes every reference in the body. The trash zone only appears while you are actively dragging a thumbnail.

Each tile shows an icon for the file type and the filename. Files already linked in the body look normal. Files that exist on disk but aren't referenced yet show a small blue dot and an "unlinked" count in the tab header.
Drag a tile into the editor to insert a link to it at the cursor.

Drag a file tile to the red trash zone at the bottom-right of the editor. ResearchOS asks for confirmation, then deletes the file from disk and strips every link to it from the markdown body, including links that stored the filename URL-encoded (so gets cleaned up when the und...

Text-like files (markdown, txt, csv, json, code, sequence files like fasta and gbk) get a popup with Cancel , Download , and View buttons. Click View and the contents render in an inline monospace viewer with its own Download button up top. Click Download from either spot and ...
PDFs get the same popup. Click View and ResearchOS opens the PDF in a new browser tab so you can use the browser's built-in PDF viewer. Click Download and a copy lands on disk.
Everything else (zips, office docs, audio, video, binaries) downloads immediately without a popup. There's nothing meaningful to render inline.
Tables with the -and- syntax.
Bulleted ( ) and numbered ( ) lists.
Task lists with and . Boxes are clickable in Edit and Preview.
Blockquotes ( ).
Horizontal rules ( ).
Inline code with single backticks and code blocks with triple backticks.
Shortcuts lists every keyboard shortcut the editor responds to, with the key combo on the right. Read-only.

Style Guide shows example syntax for every markdown feature (headings, lists, tables, code blocks, callouts). Click any example to insert it at the cursor. Handy when you've forgotten the table syntax or want to see what callout markdown looks like.

Undo / redo across the whole document . The editor maintains its own undo stack. Cmd + Z steps back through edits, including image drops and deletions. Cmd + Shift + Z (or Ctrl + Y on Windows) steps forward.
Promote/demote a heading in place with Cmd + Alt + + or Cmd + Alt + - . You don't need to retype the marks.

Focus mode ( Cmd + Shift + F or the toolbar expand glyph) gives you a full-screen, distraction-free writing view with a compact top bar. That top bar carries a width control with four presets (Narrow, Comfortable, Wide, Full-bleed) so you can set how wide the text column runs,...
The Attachments button in the toolbar toggles the bottom attachment strip (its Images and Files tabs) on and off. Click it again to bring the strip back.

entries.title: Template Library
headings: What a template is, and why
Verify any number against the original
Not every template, and that is honest
bodySnippets: A blank protocol is a slow way to start. The Template Library ships a set of pre-built, ready-to-use protocols, from a Q5 PCR recipe to a 384-well viability plate to an LC-MS run, that you can drop straight into your Methods library and edit. Many of them carry the vendor PDF ...

A template is a starting point for a method . It is a complete, structured protocol someone has already written, with the right method type (PCR, plate layout, LC gradient, free-form markdown), the reagent table filled in, the cycling program set, and the steps written out. Wh...

The point is twofold. First comes speed. You should not have to retype a standard agarose-gel procedure or rebuild a colony-PCR program from memory every time. Second, and just as important, comes fidelity . A transcribed protocol from a known source is less error-prone than o...
The library ships 91 templates grouped by the kind of lab task they cover. Each one targets a specific method type, so it opens in the right editor with the right fields the moment you use it.

This is what makes these templates trustworthy and not only convenient. A growing subset of the library, 52 of the 91 templates today and rolling out further, bundles the actual vendor document the protocol was transcribed from, whether that is the kit insert, the manufacturer...

Two buttons in the template detail make that comparison one click each. View full protocol opens the complete extracted method rendered the way it will look once it is in your library, the full reagent table and cycling program rather than the compact summary the preview shows...
Templates live one step away from your own Methods library . You browse them, preview them, and pull the one you want into your library as an editable method.

Plate-layout templates are not just a picture of a grid. They are an interactive, annotated layout for the exact plate format you work in, including 384-well plates (16 rows A through P by 24 columns). The 384-well viability template, for example, ships with the blank, vehicle...

A few of the LC-MS templates are combinations , packaging a liquid-chromatography gradient and a mass spec acquisition method into one attachable unit, because in practice the two are run together and the parameters are tuned as a pair. Three of these LC-MS combination templat...

Everything about a template is plain, inspectable data. The protocol body is markdown, the structured fields are JSON, and the bundled source PDFs are ordinary PDFs recorded with their origin URL and checksum. A method you create from a template is stored the same open way as ...
Molecular biology is the largest group, with PCR setups (Q5, Taq, colony-PCR screens), gel electrophoresis, cloning and assembly steps, and similar bench protocols.
qPCR covers dye-based and probe-based master-mix protocols (Luna, QuantiNova, and others), each with the primer concentrations, cycling, and melt-curve guidance from the kit handbook.
Cell culture covers passaging schedules, media, and viability assays.
LC-MS covers liquid-chromatography gradients and mass spec acquisition, including a few that pair the two into one unit (see LC-MS combination templates ).
Plate layouts are ready-to-annotate 96- and 384-well grids for dose-response and assay work.
Protein biochemistry, cell biology, analytical chemistry, and a general blank-protocol skeleton round out the set.

From the Methods library, open the template browser. Use the segmented control to switch between your method types and the template catalog, and the category filter and search box (by name, type, or tag) to narrow the list.

Select a template to preview it in the detail pane. A compact preview shows the shape of the protocol, and two buttons open the full view, View full protocol for the complete rendered method and (for the 52 with a bundled document) View vendor PDF for the original.
Pick a destination category for where the new method should land in your library (the detail pane shows "Will be added to" so there is no surprise), then click Use template .

The template is copied into your Methods library as a brand-new method you own. Edit it freely, attach it to experiments, and share it with the lab if you like. None of that touches the original template.

Transcription can introduce errors, and a protocol you cannot check is a protocol you have to trust blindly. With the bundled PDF one click away, you can confirm an annealing temperature, a reagent volume, or a primer concentration against the manufacturer's own insert before ...

Source-PDF bundling is a rollout, not a finished state. 52 of the 91 templates carry their document today. Templates without a bundled PDF still cite their source in the protocol text (a URL and a reference), so you always know where a procedure came from, even when the docume...
The Template Library picker. Cards are grouped by lab-task category, with a method-type pill and a search box that filters by name, type, or tag.

The Template Library picker showing template cards grouped by lab-task category, each card with a title, a short description, a method-type pill, and a small badge marking templates that bundle a source PDF.
The structured template beside the vendor PDF it came from. Any value in the template can be checked against the original.
A template detail with its bundled source PDF open side by side, showing the transcribed reagent table next to the same values in the original vendor insert.
A template detail. Preview the full protocol, choose where it lands, then Use template to copy it into your library.
A template detail pane showing the structured protocol body, the method-type pill, the destination-category control reading Will be added to, and the Use template button.
A 384-well plate-layout template. Control columns, blanks, and a dose series come pre-annotated; you adjust from there.
A 384-well plate-layout template rendered as a 16-by-24 interactive grid, with control columns and a dose-response series annotated by color.

entries.title: Methods Library
headings: What you see on the page
Sharing a method with the lab
Attach a method to an experiment
Recording variations on a single experiment
Transient read access when a task is shared with you
bodySnippets: A method is a reusable protocol you write once and attach to any experiment as a tab. The library ships ten method types, from free-form Markdown to specialized structured editors for PCR, LC gradients, plate layouts, and more, so a method matches the shape of the work instead...

The Method Library page lists every protocol you and your lab have saved, grouped under category headings (e.g., "Molecular Biology", "Imaging"). Each method is a card with its name, a colored type pill (Markdown, PDF, PCR, LC Gradient, Plate Layout, and so on), and any tags y...
Two buttons sit in the top-right of the page: + New Category creates an empty category heading you can drag methods into later, and + New Method opens the create-method modal.
The create-method modal groups types into two sections. Each type gets its own viewer when the method is opened, its own icon on experiment tabs, and its own colored pill on the library card.
Standard methods are body-only, with no structured fields beyond title and tags.
Structured methods swap the markdown body for a purpose-built editor.

You do not have to start from a blank protocol. ResearchOS ships a Template Library of pre-built, ready-to-use protocols, from a Q5 PCR recipe to a 384-well viability plate to an LC-MS run. Browse the catalog, preview a template, and copy it into your own library as a fully ed...

Categories are flat (no sub-folders). To move a method into a different category, grab its card by the ⋮⋮ handle and drop it on another category heading. The drop target highlights blue while you hover. Drop a card on the "Drop here to move to Uncategorized" bar at the top to ...
Use + New Category to set up an empty bucket before you have anything to put in it. Empty categories persist in your browser until at least one method lives there or you remove it.

Every method is private to you by default. To make one visible to the rest of the lab, open the method and click the Private pill in the header. The share popup opens. In the User dropdown, select All Lab Users . A confirmation box appears below. Click Apply . The pill flips f...
Lab-shared methods show a green Public badge on their card and appear in every user's library.

Sharing is stored on the method's array. Choosing All Lab Users writes the value into the array, which the permission system expands to "every user in this lab folder, present and future." Read access is gated by the primitive: owner, anyone explicitly in , the sentinel, or a ...

Sharing a method does not lock the creator out of it. Editing is gated by , which always passes for the original owner regardless of sharing status, so you keep your inline Edit button on a lab-shared method just like a private one. This holds for every type, Markdown and the ...
All users can always attach a shared method to their own experiments and record per-run variations without touching the shared library copy.

There is one more way a method becomes readable. When a user shares a task with you and that task references a method, you get a transient read on the underlying protocol even if the method itself is not in your . The check lives in (in ) and the method owner sees a entry land...
Inside an experiment popup, methods appear as a row of browser-style tabs at the top of the Methods area. Click the + button on the tab bar to open the method picker.

The picker has a search box at the top (search by name or ), a Recently used in this project section pinned to the top, and a folder-grouped list below. Hover or arrow through entries to preview the method body in the right pane, then press Enter or click to attach.

When you open a method tab on an experiment, the amber Variation Notes bar sits at the top. Expand it and click + Add Note to add a timestamped entry where you can describe what you did differently for this run (e.g., "halved the elongation time", "substituted Q5 for Phusion")...

Most method types also let you edit the protocol itself directly inside the experiment tab, not just leave a note about it. A PCR tab edits its thermal gradient and recipe table, an LC tab its gradient, a Plate tab its layout, and the Cell Culture, qPCR Analysis, and Markdown ...
PDF methods are the exception. They render the original file and have no per-experiment copy, so the Variation Notes panel is the only place to record what you did differently for that run.

Markdown is the default. Free-form protocol text in the same markdown editor used for lab notes and results, with toolbars, image drag-drop, and live preview. See The Markdown Editor for the full shortcut set.
PDF lets you upload an existing protocol PDF (a kit insert, a published paper). PDFs render in an iframe and can't be edited inline.
PCR opens a thermal-gradient editor and reaction-recipe table. Full details on the PCR Reaction Builder page.
LC Gradient opens a solvent-gradient chart editor with flow, column, and mobile-phase fields for HPLC and LC-MS protocols.
Plate Layout renders an interactive well-plate grid with sample, control, and blank annotations.
Cell culture passaging tracks a passaging schedule, media, and cell line, with per-task passage history.
Mass spec captures ionization mode, source and scan params, and calibration. Pairs with LC for LC-MS workflows.
qPCR analysis records Cq readouts, melt-curve Tm, standard-curve efficiency, and ΔΔCq fold-change.
Coding workflow stores reusable scripts (Python, R, SQL) and Jupyter notebooks alongside protocol text.
Kit bundles existing methods into one attachable unit (e.g. plate layout plus assay PDF). Reached by extending an existing method, not as a standalone picker tile.

Click + New Method . Pick a type from the Standard or Structured section and give it a name. The Folder (optional) field autocompletes against existing categories (the page-level headings) so methods drop into the right bucket.

Fill in the body. Write markdown, upload a PDF, draw an LC gradient, lay out a plate, or build a PCR program and recipe. For markdown methods you can drag images and attachment files directly into the editor.
Click Create Method . The new card lands in its category on the Method Library page and is immediately available to attach to experiments.
The picker's search box matches names and tags (prefix with to search tags only). Many labs end up with five slightly different copies of the same protocol. A quick search prevents that.
The Method Library, where cards are grouped under category headings with type pills on each card.
The Methods page showing categories of method cards, with type pills marking the method type on each card.

entries.title: Notifications & Inbox
headings: Right-click a single row to skip the active-experiment dance
The toast is your fastest path to filing
bodySnippets: The bell, the photo inbox, and event reminders. All three live in the app shell, so you see new things wherever you're working.
ResearchOS surfaces new things in three places.
Click the bell to drop down a list of notifications. Rows come in several kinds, each with its own icon. The three most common are below, and the lab-specific kinds follow.

Clicking anywhere on an unread row marks it as read but doesn't go anywhere. Clicking a row that is already read is a no-op. Each row also has a blue pill with a checkmark on the right that marks just that one item as read, so you can silence a single notification without touc...

At the top of the dropdown, Mark all read clears the unread badge without removing anything. It only appears when at least one unread item exists. Clear read removes the rows you've already acknowledged, and only appears when at least one read item is present. Clear all emptie...

The bell rechecks for new notifications every 30 seconds while the app is open. When something fires inside this tab (e.g., a calendar reminder), the badge updates immediately rather than waiting for the next poll. Shift alerts work differently. Another lab member writes them ...

Click the Inbox pill to open a centered popup with two tabs. The Shared with me tab holds items another ResearchOS user sent you across folders (see Sharing & Permissions for that flow), and the Photos tab, covered below, holds images waiting to be filed. Each photo row shows ...
Each row carries these actions on the right.
Clicking a single row opens an image editor where you can rename the file, edit the caption, see when it was received, and either move it to the active experiment or delete it.
For a stack of photos that all belong to the same experiment, the modal supports multi-select so you don't have to file them one at a time.

With one or more rows selected, right-click any selected row (or click the ⋯ button) to open a context menu. The top two items read Send to task... and Send to note... for a single row, or Send N items to task... / Send N items to note... when multiple are selected. Picking ei...

If any filename in the batch collides with an existing image in the destination task, the duplicate-resolution dialog pops up for each collision so you can Rename , Replace , or Cancel per item. Non-colliding items go through without interruption. When the batch finishes, a gr...

The moment a new photo arrives in your inbox, a toast appears in the bottom-right corner with a thumbnail, the caption, and a one-click action. If an experiment popup is open, the action is File here , which moves the photo straight into that experiment. If nothing's open, the...

Toasts auto-dismiss after about 12 seconds, and any photo you file or delete elsewhere also drops out of the toast queue automatically. The small close button on a toast just dismisses the notification, the photo stays in the inbox until you act on it.

The bell handles things you need to acknowledge directly, including the @-mentions and lab notifications above. The broader comment stream lives in a separate surface, the Lab Inbox . The two are kept separate on purpose.

The split is by urgency. A direct @-mention lands in the bell so you do not miss a callout, while general comments on records you can read stay in the Lab Inbox only. See Comments for the threading and mention model.
Reminders are a single setting that applies to every timed event on your calendar. Open the Calendar page and click Reminders at the top of the left sidebar. The popup holds three things.

With reminders on, every timed event in the next 24 hours is queued. When the chosen lead time hits, a row appears in the bell and (if you've granted browser permission) an OS notification pops on top of whatever you're doing. All-day events don't produce reminders, since ther...

Reminder settings, the bell history, and the photo inbox are all per-user. If you share a folder with the rest of your lab, everyone has their own bell, their own inbox, and their own reminder preferences.

The bell in the top-right of the header collects everything that needs your attention. Things someone shared with you (tasks, methods, projects), calendar reminders that have fired, shift alerts when a labmate reschedules a shared task, @-mentions in comments, and (in a lab) a...

The Inbox pill , right next to the bell, opens a centered modal with two tabs, Shared with me (items another ResearchOS user sent you across folders) and Photos (images waiting to be filed into an experiment). The pill turns amber and shows a count that sums both when anything...
An inbox toast appears at the bottom-right corner the moment a new photo arrives, so you can file it in one click without hunting for the modal.
Shared with you. When another lab member shares a task, method, or project with you, a row appears here showing who shared it, what it was, and whether you got view or edit access.
Event reminder. When a calendar reminder you set up fires (see below), a row lands here with the event title, the time it starts, and how many minutes you asked to be warned in advance.

Shift alert. When another lab member with edit access reschedules a task you also have access to, a row appears reading "Alex shifted PCR optimization by +3d" with the old and new start dates underneath. The amber delta covers start-date moves and, when start and end shifted b...
@-mentions. When someone @-mentions you by name in a comment, that mention surfaces here as its own row so a direct callout doesn't get lost in the ambient comment feed.

Lab notifications. Inside a lab, the bell also fires for announcements, task assignments your PI hands you, purchase approvals, flag-for-review notes on your records, and purchase-order updates (someone asked you to order a supply, or yours was ordered). Each lands as its own ...

Move to active. Sends the photo into whichever surface is currently open in ResearchOS, whether an experiment, a note, or (if both popups are open) a small dropdown lets you pick between them. The button is disabled when nothing is open, with a tooltip explaining why.

⋯ (more actions). A small ellipsis button that only fades in when you hover the row. Clicking it opens the same menu as right-click, anchored under the button, with Send to task... , Send to note... , Move to active , and Delete .
Delete. Removes the photo from the inbox folder (with a confirm prompt).
Shift-click a second row to select the contiguous range from your last anchor row to this one.
Cmd-click (or Ctrl-click on Windows / Linux) to toggle individual rows in and out of the selection without disturbing the rest.
Selected rows pick up a blue border and a blue ring so you can see the group at a glance. Clicking the empty area of the modal clears the selection.
The bell pings you for things you need to acknowledge, including @-mentions, shares, fired reminders, shift alerts, and lab notifications.
The Lab Inbox is ambient. Comments people left on your tasks and notes appear there, alongside the lab-wide announcement stream and the @-mention filter.
A master Enable reminders checkbox.
A Remind me dropdown for how far in advance to fire (1 minute, 5, 10, 15, 30, 1 hour, 2 hours, or 1 day before).
A button to grant your browser permission to show OS-level popups, plus a Send test link once it's granted.

Right-click any inbox row (no multi-select needed) and pick Send to task... to send that one photo to any experiment, even one you don't have open. It's the fastest way to file a photo into the right experiment without first opening that experiment's popup.
If a photo lands while you're working in an experiment, the toast lets you attach it in one click without opening the Inbox modal at all.

Because ResearchOS has no server, reminders only fire while a ResearchOS tab is open in your browser. If you close every tab, the in-app row still appears the next time you open the app, but no live OS popup will go off in the meantime. For phone-side or always-on-but-laptop-a...
The bell and the Inbox pill sit together in the top-right of the header, next to the help and settings icons.
The notification bell with a count badge and the Inbox pill button beside it in the top-right of the app header.
Shift-alert row in the bell. The amber delta is the headline. View task jumps to the new start date on the calendar, Ignore dismisses the row.
A shift-alert row in the bell dropdown reading 'Alex shifted PCR optimization by +3d', with old and new start dates underneath, and View task and Ignore buttons.
Open the Inbox to see waiting photos. Each row has a Move-to-active and Delete button, and clicking the row opens an editor for the caption and filename.
The Inbox modal showing waiting photos with thumbnails, captions, timestamps, and per-row Move and Delete buttons.
Shift-click or Cmd-click to select multiple inbox photos, then right-click for the batch menu.
The Inbox modal with three rows selected (blue borders) and the right-click context menu showing Send 3 items to task, Move to active, and Delete.

entries.title: Mentoring and check-ins
headings: Who sets it up and who sees it
A 1:1 is not a shared notebook
bodySnippets: A 1:1 is a private advising workspace shared between a lab head and one member. Both people type into the same four areas (weekly goals, meeting notes, freeform notes, and a running agenda), so the record of an advising relationship lives in one place instead of scattered acro...

A 1:1 is one shared space per lab-head and member pair. It is not a notebook and it does not appear in the Notes rail. It is a purpose-built advising workspace that both people edit, so the weekly goals you agreed on, the notes from your last meeting, and the agenda for the ne...

Each 1:1 is shared with exactly the two people in it. Everything you add inside is visible to both of you and to no one else (a PI's implicit view-all still applies, the same as everywhere else in ResearchOS). See Sharing and permissions for how that read access works.
The label is role-relative, so it always names the other person. The same underlying record reads differently depending on who is looking.
There is no fixed "1:1s" label anywhere in the app. The framing derives from your identity, so a lab head always gets Mentoring and a member always gets Check-ins for the same shared space.
Every 1:1 has the same four areas, shown as sub-tabs in the main pane. Both people can add to and edit all four.

The lab head creates a 1:1 and picks the member it is with, using the "Start a new 1:1" action on the Mentoring tab. The member then sees that 1:1 appear under their Check-ins tab automatically. Access is symmetric from that point on.

The tab itself is gated so no one stares at an empty surface. A lab head always sees the Mentoring tab (so they can set the first one up). Everyone else only sees the Check-ins tab once they are actually in at least one 1:1. A solo user with no lab head and no 1:1s never sees ...
The 1:1 surface is the Mentoring (or Check-ins) tab on the Workbench , alongside Projects, Experiments, Notes, and Lists. If you do not see the tab, it is because the gate above is hiding it.
The lab head sees the Mentoring framing. The Workbench tab reads "Mentoring" and each entry is labeled by the member, for example "Alex - Mentoring".
The member sees the Check-ins framing. The tab reads "Check-ins" and each entry is labeled by the lab head, for example "Dr. Lee - Check-ins".

Weekly goals. A per-week checklist either person can add to and check off. A week selector moves between weeks, so the goals you set in one meeting stay attached to that week. The typical flow is the lab head assigns and the member checks off, but both can do either.
Meeting notes. A shared running log, one entry per meeting, with a date picker so each entry is filed under the meeting it belongs to. Both people type into the same log.
Notes. Freeform shared notes scoped to this 1:1, for anything that is not a goal or a meeting note.
Agenda. A running list of agenda and action items for the next meeting. Items carry between meetings until they are checked off, so nothing falls through.
The Workbench covers the other tabs the 1:1 sits next to, including shared notebooks.
PI covers the lab-head role that unlocks the Mentoring tab by default.
Sharing and permissions explains the read and write access that backs every shared 1:1.

These are two independent things. A shared notebook is a plain container of notes that any two or more people can share, with no weekly goals, meetings, or agenda. A 1:1 is the structured advising workspace with all four areas. A lab head and a member can have both at the same...
A lab head's view. The left rail lists one row per mentee, and the main pane carries the four shared areas.
The 1:1 surface, with a left list of the viewer's 1:1s and a main pane carrying Weekly goals, Meeting notes, Notes, and Agenda tabs.

entries.title: PCR Protocols
headings: The four zones of a program
Build a protocol step by step
Attaching a protocol to an experiment
Checking off ingredients at the bench
bodySnippets: Save reusable PCR programs (temperature steps plus reagent mix) and attach them to experiments the same way you attach a method.
Each protocol opens in a popup with two panels stacked on top of each other.
It is the same library-plus-variation pattern as Methods . ResearchOS keeps the saved protocol in your library and copies its gradient and reagent values onto each experiment that attaches it.

Reading the gradient panel left to right, every protocol moves through four zones in this order. The default that a brand-new protocol starts with shows all four, so a new protocol is also the quickest way to see the layout.

Block color is keyed to temperature across six bands, blue (≤ 15 °C), cyan (≤ 30 °C), green (≤ 50 °C), amber (≤ 70 °C), orange (≤ 85 °C), and red (above 85 °C). A step typed in at the wrong temperature is visually obvious because its color doesn't match its neighbors.

From an experiment popup, the Attach a method picker shows every method and protocol in your library, your own and any in the public folder. Pick a PCR protocol and ResearchOS copies the gradient and the reagent table onto that experiment.

From that point on, the experiment carries its own copy. Bump the annealing temperature by a degree, add another microliter of primer, knock the cycle count down to 28 for a low-template run. The experiment records the variation and the library protocol stays exactly as you le...

When you open a PCR protocol on an experiment (via the Method tab), the Reaction Recipe table gains a checkoff column on the left. Each ingredient row has a small checkbox. Tick it as you pipette each reagent into your tube. Checked rows turn green and a progress bar at the to...

Protocols are stored under your user by default and visible only to you. The API supports a public flag that would write to , but the creation UI does not expose this toggle . Sharing a PCR protocol is currently backend-only. The picker on each experiment shows protocols from ...

The Thermal Gradient panel up top is a left-to-right row of colored square blocks, one per step. Each block shows the temperature and duration of that step. A purple dashed rectangle wraps the cycled section and carries a small badge for the repeat count.

The Reaction Recipe panel below has one row per ingredient with a stock concentration and a per-reaction volume in microliters. The bottom Total row is a manual entry where you type the target reaction volume. It looks identical to the ingredient rows above it (no input border...
An initial step is one or more blocks that run once before the cycled section. Typically this is initial denaturation around 95 °C for three minutes, painted red.

The cycled block is a group of blocks wrapped in a purple dashed rectangle, repeated end-to-end by the count on the purple badge above it (30 by default). The usual three are denaturation, annealing, and extension. Steps inside this block carry a faint purple ring so you can t...
A final step is one or more blocks after the cycled block that run once. Typically final extension at 72 °C for five minutes.
The hold is a single block at the right end with the duration Indef. , parking the thermocycler at a low temperature (12 °C by default) until you come back to it. Painted blue.
Go to Methods and click + New Method . In the type picker that opens, choose the PCR type. That drops you into the protocol editor. Type a name into the Method Name field at the top of the popup.

The gradient panel opens with a sensible default loaded in (initial denaturation, the three-step cycle at 30 repeats, final extension, and a hold). Click the Edit Cycle button on the toolbar to enter editing mode. The blocks start jiggling to signal that they are now interactive.

Edit any block. Double-click a block to open the Edit Step popup. It has three fields, Step Name (e.g. Annealing ), Temperature (°C) as a number input, and Duration as free text (e.g. 20 sec , 2 min ). The Hold checkbox next to the duration field locks duration to Indef. for t...

Reorder. Single-click a block to select it (a blue ring appears) and a pair of ← → arrow buttons drop in above the block. The arrows swap the block with its neighbor. The arrows step around the cycled block rather than into it. A block outside the cycle stays outside, and a bl...

Move a step in or out of the cycle. When a block inside the cycled rectangle is selected, a red Remove from Cycle button appears next to the arrows. When a block outside the cycle is selected, a purple Add to Cycle button appears with a dropdown listing each cycle by position ...
Adjust the repeat count. Click the purple badge above the cycled rectangle to open the Edit Cycle Repeats popup. Type a new number (1–100) and save.

Add or remove blocks. The toolbar has + Add Step (opens a StepEditPopup pre-populated with "New Step", 60 °C, 30 sec, where the block only lands in the gradient after you click Save ) and + Add Cycle (opens an Add Empty Cycle confirmation modal with Cancel and Add buttons, whe...
Hit Done Editing to leave editing mode. The jiggle stops and the toolbar collapses.

Scroll down to Reaction Recipe . Fill in each row with a stock concentration (e.g. 10x ) and a per-reaction volume in microliters. Use + Add Row at the bottom of the table to add ingredients, and the x on each row to remove one. The Total row at the bottom is a manual entry. T...

Type any free-text Notes (lot numbers, master mix quirks, the day's primer pair). Click Create Method . The card appears in your protocol library and shows up in the Attach a method picker on any experiment.

Same model as Methods. When a PCR protocol is attached to an experiment, any edits to volumes, temperatures, or cycle counts apply only to that run. The library copy stays untouched for the next person who attaches it.
The protocol editor, with the thermal gradient on top and the reagent table below.
The PCR protocol editor with a row of colored temperature blocks on top and a reagent table below.
Double-click any block to open the Edit Step popup.
The Edit Step popup with fields for step name, temperature, and duration, plus a Hold checkbox.
The recipe table sits below the gradient. The Total row at the bottom is a manual entry that holds your target reaction volume.
The Reaction Recipe table with rows for each ingredient and a checkoff checkbox column, plus a Total row at the bottom.

entries.title: Project Surface
headings: The project card on the Workbench
bodySnippets: A project has two faces. A compact card on the Workbench Projects tab for quick stats and a way in, and a full route page that hosts the project's hypothesis prose, results, methods, sequences, goals, activity, and funding.

Your projects live on the Workbench , in a responsive grid on the Projects tab. That tab is the default landing view, so a member who opens ResearchOS sees their projects first, not a flat task list. Each project is a single card. From top to bottom, a card carries four things.

The card has no drag handle and no menu button. Clicking anywhere on the card navigates to the project's route at . There is no popup intermediate step. Project-level actions (rename, share, archive, delete) live on the route's top bar, covered below.

The route opens with a color stripe across the top edge, then a sticky top bar. The top bar carries a ← Projects breadcrumb back to the grid, the project name, and (on the right) a View timeline -> link that jumps to the Gantt prefiltered to this project, plus a row of icon bu...

Below the top bar sits a row of tabs . These are real tabs backed by app state, not scroll anchors, so only the active section's content renders at a time. The full set is Overview , Results , Methods , Sequences , Goals , Activity , and Funding , but a tab only appears when i...
The Overview is where you write the project's hypothesis, motivation, and big-picture context. It is a plain resizable textarea with no toolbar, no image-paste, and no drag-drop file attachment.

Edits autosave 1.5 seconds after the last keystroke. A small Saving... / Saved indicator near the section title tells you when the write has landed. The text lives in a sidecar file at , so it is portable, greppable, and editable outside the app.

The Results section pulls every image from every Results tab on every experiment that belongs to this project, then groups them by experiment. Each group has a collapsible header with the experiment name and the image count. Within a group, thumbnails are sorted newest-first. ...

Experiments hosted into this project by labmates (their experiments, attached to your project) appear in their own groups with a Shared by <owner> chip. Hosted groups are suppressed on archived projects.

The Methods section is a flat, deduplicated inventory of every method attached to an experiment in this project. Each row carries the method name, a type pill (Markdown, PDF, PCR), and a used in N experiments badge so you can see which protocols this project actually leans on.

Rows are sorted by usage count descending, with the method name as an alphabetical tiebreaker. Click any row to jump to the full method in the Method Library . If the same method appears on hosted experiments (from labmates), you'll see a via <owner> chip next to the row.

The Sequences section lists the plasmids and sequences from your sequence library that are linked to this project. Each row shows the sequence name, its length in base pairs, and a type pill (DNA, RNA, or Protein). The tab appears only when at least one sequence is linked, so ...

This is a read-only roll-up, not the editor. Clicking a row, or the Manage in the sequence library -> link in the section header, takes you to the full sequence library where you map, annotate, and edit. To link a sequence to a project, set its project membership in that library.

If you turned Goals on during the Welcome Wizard , a Goals tab appears just before Activity. It lists every high-level goal whose matches this project, with active goals on top and completed ones below. Each row shows the goal's color dot, name, date range, and SMART sub-goal ...
Click a row to open the goal editor. If you skipped Goals during the wizard, the tab is hidden entirely. You can opt in later from Settings .

Activity is a newest-first feed of events scoped to this project. The feed picks up task completions, image drops on any of the project's experiments, methods attached or removed, overview edits, share events, and archive flips. Each row carries an icon, a one-line summary, an...
Events live in a sidecar at , which means every collaborator on a shared project sees the same feed. Events older than 90 days are lazily pruned the next time the file is written.
The Funding section ties a project to the grants that pay for it, which is the backbone of an accurate data-management or grant report. It shows two complementary things.

The section is read-only and self-hides when a project has no primary link and no charged grants, so an unfunded project stays uncluttered. For how the grant metadata flows into a deposit and a compliant report, see NIH data-management compliance .
The project route's sticky top bar holds the project-level actions as icon buttons on the right. Hover any icon for a tooltip naming it.
When a labmate shares a project with you, the URL gains an query parameter so the route knows which user's files to read.
How much you can change depends on the permission you were granted.
On the route, a small Shared by <owner> chip sits next to the project name so you always know whose namespace you're reading from.
ResearchOS ships with a built-in Miscellaneous project for standalone tasks that don't belong to a specific research project. It behaves differently from a real project.
A "Shared by" pill at the very top, but only on cards for projects a labmate shared with you. Your own cards skip it.

Color dot and name. A small filled dot in the project's chosen color sits next to the project name. A project you never picked a color for falls back to one from a built-in palette, and you can change it any time from the Edit dialog.

Completion bar. A thin bar showing the percentage of this project's tasks that are complete, with an N of M complete label and a percentage above it. A project with no tasks yet reads No tasks yet at 0 percent.
Count chips. A row of small chips at the bottom counting experiments , list tasks , and (when the project has any) sequences .

Primary grant. The single funding account you linked to the project in the Edit dialog. This is the project's declared funding source. When nothing is linked, the section reads No primary grant linked .

Grants charged in this project. The distinct set of grants that purchases inside the project were actually charged to, derived live from each purchase's funding line. This can differ from the primary grant, which is the whole point. It surfaces where the money really went. A c...

Version history opens a side panel of saved versions. Pick one to preview a read-only diff, and (when you can write the project) restore it. After a restore an Undo restore button appears in the top bar for a 24-hour window.
Edit opens the edit dialog, where you can rename the project, change its color, edit tags, toggle the 7-day schedule, and link a funding account. Disabled for view-only receivers.
Share opens the share dialog so you can grant a labmate access. Hidden entirely on projects shared to you, since only the original owner can grant access.
Deposit to a repository opens the deposit dialog for publishing the project's outputs to a data repository.

Archive / Unarchive triggers an amber confirmation before archiving. Archived projects keep all their tasks but drop out of the active grid and the Gantt. Unarchive from the same button. Disabled for view-only receivers.
Delete is owner-only. It is disabled for any receiver, view or edit permission. Only the original owner can destroy the project file.

View permission. The Edit and Archive buttons render disabled (cursor-not-allowed), and the Share button is omitted entirely. The route still loads, and you can read the overview, browse the results gallery, scan the methods and sequences inventories, and read the activity fee...

Edit permission. The Edit and Archive actions are live. Your writes route back to the owner's directory, so they reflect on their copy too. Delete is still owner-only, grayed out for any receiver regardless of permission.
Its route top bar hides every action button . Edit, Share, Deposit, Archive, and Delete are not available, because Miscellaneous can't be renamed, shared, archived, or removed.
Clicking its card on the grid navigates to the route at , where you can see progress and the activity feed.

The Overview and Results tabs still render (those two always show), but there is no project hypothesis to write up and no Methods or Sequences inventory to deduplicate, since Miscellaneous holds loose tasks rather than experiments and constructs.
Open the Projects tab on the Workbench (it's where you land by default) to see the grid of project cards.
Click a card to navigate to that project's route. The card itself has no menu, project-level actions live on the route's top bar.
On the route, write into the Overview textarea and use the tab strip to move between Results, Methods, Sequences, optional Goals, Activity, and Funding. Only the tabs with content show.
Use the ← Projects breadcrumb in the top bar to return to the grid.
Your own projects live at . A project a labmate has shared with you appends an owner hint: . That second segment is how ResearchOS picks the right per-user file path when ids collide across labmates.

You will not see a Methods or Sequences tab until the project actually has an attached method or a linked sequence. The tab strip grows as the project fills in, which keeps an early-stage project from looking broken. Overview and Results are the exception, they always show.

A built-in project called Miscellaneous holds standalone tasks that don't belong to a research project. Its route top bar hides every action button (edit, share, deposit, archive, delete), and it can't be renamed or removed. The route still works as a read-only progress view.
Miscellaneous holds ad-hoc tasks and can't be edited, shared, archived, or deleted. Its route still works as a read-only progress view.

Project routes are first-class URLs. Bookmark them, paste them into your lab notebook, or drop the link into a Slack thread. Anyone with view-or-edit access on a shared project lands on the same view when they open the URL.
The project route at /workbench/projects/1, opened on the Overview tab.
The project route page with a color stripe, a breadcrumb back to Projects, the project name, top-bar action icons, and a row of tabs reading Overview Results Methods.
Results are grouped by experiment, newest images first.
The Results section of the project route, showing two experiment groups each with their own header row and a strip of thumbnail images.
The Methods inventory deduplicates across experiments and counts usage.
The Methods section of the project route, listing several methods with type pills and a 'used in N experiments' badge on each row.
Activity is a chronological feed scoped to this project.
The Activity section, listing a chronological feed of events. Each row has an icon, a summary line, and a relative timestamp on the right.

entries.title: Purchases & Funding
headings: Cost transparency, not a filing cabinet
Vendor and category as first-class fields
The loose model and the soft warning
Breakdown by project, vendor, or category
Linking a project to a grant
Moving to Ordered notifies the requester
bodySnippets: Track every dollar your lab spends and check grant burn in one view. The Purchases page pairs a flat reverse-chronological list of every purchase order with a live spending dashboard, so you can scan recent activity and check budget burn without leaving the page.

A purchase order in ResearchOS is a task with its task_type set to . Each row on the page is one of those tasks, and inside each row is a small spreadsheet of line items (the actual things you bought). The page is built around lookup, not data entry. Lab purchase lifecycles ar...

The header at the top reads Purchases · N orders · $X.XX total , where the total comes from every line item across every order visible to you. To the right is the Manage Funding Accounts button, which opens a popup where you can add or edit grant codes without le...

The amber + New Purchase button in the top-right of the page header opens . The modal collapses the two-step data model (parent purchase task + first line item) into a single form, so the common case of logging one item takes one interaction instead of two.

Reordering lives right here in this flow, not in a separate floating button anywhere else in the app. Above the Item Name field is a recently ordered row, a few one-tap chips for the items you bought most recently, newest first. Tap one and the modal pre-fills the name, vendor...
The modal has six fields.

On save, the modal creates a parent task dated today with a one-day duration, then immediately creates the first line item under it. After saving you can expand the row in the list and use the inline PurchaseEditor to add more line items to the same order.

Above the order list are two rows of chips. The first is a category segmented control with four chips that scope what you see. All shows every purchase task and is always visible. Project purchases filters to orders attached to a real project, hiding the Miscellaneous bucket. ...

Below the category chips is a separate Ordering chip row that filters by where each order sits in its lifecycle (Any stage, Needs ordering, Ordered, Received). It composes with the category chips above, and the lifecycle itself is described in the next section.

The list itself is a single column sorted by start date, newest first. There is no active vs earlier split. A completed order looks almost identical to an in-flight one, with the same row, the same colors, a small green dot, and the text appended to the metadata line. The row ...

Click any row to expand it into a line-item table. Each row is one thing you are buying with columns for item name, quantity, vendor link, price per unit, shipping, computed total, the funding account paying for it, plus the Vendor and Category columns. A line item carries two...

The existing data model requires every purchase task to have a . Purchases that do not belong to any real project, like conference flights, lab snacks, or one-off equipment, still need somewhere to land. Rather than making nullable and updating every reader (Gantt, Workbench, ...

The hidden project never appears on Home, Workbench, Gantt, the project picker, or anywhere in the app that calls without explicitly opting in. The route is the only surface that passes , so the bucket is visible (and filterable) there but invisible everywhere else.

Lab purchasing has heavy vendor reuse. The same supplier shows up across dozens of orders, often spelled three different ways. To make that data useful for analytics, every line item now carries a vendor and a category , both stored as nullable free-text strings on . They are ...

Each input is wired to a sourced from , deduplicated, non-null values only. So the first time you type into a vendor cell, the dropdown surfaces every distinct vendor you (or anyone sharing with you) has typed before. Suggestions update as new values are added elsewhere, inclu...

A purchase is not done the moment you log it. Every line item carries an ordering status that moves through three stages, Needs ordering , then Ordered , then Received . A fresh item starts at Needs ordering, and older records with no status read as Needs ordering too, so the ...

Each line item shows a small colored status chip (gray for Needs ordering, blue for Ordered, green for Received) with forward and back arrows next to it. Click the forward arrow to advance a stage or the back arrow to revert one. The control is available to any lab member, so ...

Reagents run out and get reordered on a loop. On any Received line item, a one-click Buy again control creates a brand-new purchase order that copies the item's name, vendor, CAS or accession, link, price, and quantity, and starts it back at Needs ordering . No form, no retypi...

ResearchOS does not block you from attaching a purchase item to a task whose type is not . That is on purpose. Reagents often tie to a specific experiment task, and you don't want to lose that experiment-to-spend link.

Opening a purchase editor against a non-purchase task surfaces a soft amber note above the line-item table. The system does not block you. The items will show up under Items on non-purchase tasks in the dashboard below so they stay visible.

Scroll past the order list and the page closes out with a live spending dashboard. Every number on it is recomputed from the same set of items you can see above. Funding accounts store only a budget cap, no spend counter, so each account's spent total is summed live from the l...

The top of the dashboard has two controls. The time range dropdown chooses the window, Last 30 days , Last 90 days , Last 12 months (the default), All time , or a Custom date range with from and to inputs. The All projects checkbox lets the dashboard ignore the global project ...

Below the controls is a row of cards, one per funding account. Each card shows the account name, dollars spent, total budget, dollars remaining, and a progress bar. The numbers are computed live from items in the current window. Over-budget cards turn red. Items with a funding...

Next is a vertical bar chart of monthly spend across the selected window. Empty months still render as zero-height bars so the time span on the x-axis matches your range selection. Hover any bar to see the dollar total and item count for that month.

Below the time chart is a horizontal bar chart with a segmented control in the section header to switch lenses. The same set of items in the current window is regrouped by project, by vendor, or by category. Items with no value for the active lens (no vendor set, no category s...

Near the bottom of the dashboard is a thin amber line that reads Items on non-purchase tasks: followed by a count and a dollar total. Click it to expand an inline table of those items with their host task name and amount. This panel exists because the loose model lets you legi...

The top-right of the dashboard has an Export CSV button that downloads the currently filtered scope (time range plus project filter, plus any funding-card filter you have clicked) as a flat file named . Columns are item id, item name, vendor, category, funding string, project ...

The dashboard export covers what you are looking at right now. For a grant audit, the page header has a separate Export audit CSV button that ignores the dashboard filters and writes every purchase grouped by grant, with references to each attached document, to a file named . ...

PIs have in their nav just like everyone else, and the page gives them the same order list and dashboard scoped to lab-visible spend. For a lab-wide rollup, the LabPurchases Tool inside the Lab Overview opens from the Tools launcher in the header. The popup is a four-tab dashb...

A declined purchase carries a timestamp and renders with a red wherever it appears, in the member's own list, in the All Purchases tab, and in the Lab Activity stream. The Pending Approvals tab also shows a Recently declined section at the bottom so a PI can Re-approve a previ...

Click Manage Funding Accounts at the top right and a popup opens over the page, so you can edit grants without losing your place in the list. Each account has a name (for example ), a total budget, and an optional one-line description.

Each funding account can carry the official grant details beneath its everyday label. Expand Grant details (for data sharing / DOI) on an account and you can record the structured fields a repository deposit needs.

These fields are deliberately separate from the account name. The name is your own short label that purchases match on, while the award number is the official identifier and may differ. All of them map one-to-one to the DataCite fields, so when you later deposit an experiment ...

A funding account can be linked to a project as its primary grant. That project-to-grant link is what the deposit flow reads when it prefills the funder fields, so a single experiment under that project carries the right award number and funder into its DataCite metadata autom...
A few things in this area are not yet shipped, listed here so you do not spend time looking for them.

Item Name (required): a native autocomplete surfaces every distinct item name from your purchase history, de-duped case-insensitively with the most-recent record winning. When the typed value matches a prior item name exactly, Vendor and Price per unit fill in automatically. Q...
Vendor : free text, optional. Not auto-filled unless an exact Item Name match triggers the recall logic above.

Category : a listing your non-archived, non-shared owned projects plus a synthetic Miscellaneous option at the bottom. Defaults to the first owned project alphabetically, falling back to Miscellaneous if you have no owned projects yet. Choosing Miscellaneous routes the purchas...
Price per unit and Quantity : numeric free-text inputs. Price defaults blank, and quantity defaults to 1.

Funding string : a autocomplete pulls from your existing funding accounts (for example, ). Typing a new string and saving creates a budget-zero funding account automatically so the string appears in future dropdowns without a separate setup step. The field is optional, so leav...

Pending approvals . The work queue. Each row is a purchase waiting on the PI with inline Approve and Decline buttons. The first action of a fresh session unlocks the 5-minute edit window, and subsequent rows process without re-prompting. See Edit session and password .
All purchases . A flat lab-wide view of every purchase across every member, filterable by member, project, and funding string.
Funding . The funding-account cards (spent vs. budget) rolled up across the whole lab, not just the PI's own purchases.
Spending . The full spend-over-time chart and the breakdown lenses (project / vendor / category), same shape as the dashboard at the bottom of but with lab-wide scope.
Award number : the official grant identifier (for example ). A soft hint nudges you toward the NIH shape, but any value is accepted.
Funder name : the funding body, with a datalist seeded with NIH and NSF. Choosing NIH auto-fills its funder ID and ID type.
Funder ID and funder ID type : the funder's identifier and its scheme (Crossref Funder ID, ROR, GRID, ISNI, or Other).
Award title : the grant's full title, optional.
Per-item dates (the time axis uses the parent task's start date)
A timestamp
Multi-currency support
Recurring or subscription purchases
OCR receipt import
Anomaly detection and spending alerts
Budget-threshold notifications
A catalog-prefilled vendor and category list (datalist autocomplete is the only source today)

Be careful not to conflate two distinct concepts that share the same label. The Category select in NewPurchaseModal picks which project the parent purchase task routes to, and choosing "Miscellaneous" routes it to the hidden project. Separately, the Miscellaneous filter chip o...

The free-text vendor and category columns on individual rows are distinct from the top-level project routing. The top-level routing has one reserved bucket. Miscellaneous routes to the hidden project. One is the project the order lives in, the other is a per-line-item annotati...

When a labmate placed an order on someone else's behalf, advancing that item from Needs ordering to Ordered fires a bell to the requester, so the person who asked for the reagent finds out it is on the way without having to check back. This is the real signal that the order wa...

When you have no purchases yet, the spend-over-time chart shows the prompt Add a purchase to see spend breakdowns here. The funding cards still render with a zero spent value against each account's budget, so the budget scaffold is visible from day one.

entries.title: Restriction digest
headings: From a digest to a clone
Split out of the Sequences page
bodySnippets: The restriction digest tools find where enzymes cut your sequence, what overhangs they leave, and what fragment sizes you would see on a gel.

A restriction enzyme recognizes a short, specific sequence of DNA and cuts the double helix at or near that site. Knowing where an enzyme cuts a plasmid, and how many times, is the everyday question behind picking cloning sites, designing a diagnostic digest, and reading a gel...

The digest is a display layer on the open sequence. Turn on the Enzyme sites chip in the display strip above the viewer to overlay cut sites on the circular or linear map, and open the enzyme picker (the Choose enzymes action under the Cut operation in the right sidebar) to ch...

The Workspace ships with a large catalog of restriction enzymes drawn from a standard NEB-derived dataset (over two hundred enzymes). Each enzyme carries its recognition sequence and its cut geometry, and from that the catalog derives the metadata you filter on. That covers ho...

For a given sequence and enzyme set, the digest searches the recognition site on both strands, the forward strand and the reverse complement, so a cut site is found regardless of which way the recognition sequence reads. Each cut records its position and which strand the recog...

When an enzyme cuts, the top-strand cut and the bottom-strand cut may land at the same position or at offset positions. When they coincide, the cut leaves a blunt end. When the top strand cuts before the bottom strand, the cut leaves a 5' overhang. When the top strand cuts aft...

Once the cut positions are known, the digest computes the fragment sizes you would see on a gel, sorted from largest to smallest. Topology matters here. For a linear molecule the two ends are open, so the cuts divide it into fragments with a piece at each end. For a circular m...

The enzyme picker mirrors the SnapGene chooser. You can search enzymes by name, hide enzymes that never cut the open sequence, and restrict the list by cut-count category. You can show only the enzymes that cut exactly once (unique cutters), or those that cut exactly N times, ...

Alongside the manual filters, a few computed presets give you a one-click set that is always derived from the current sequence. Common is the everyday workhorse set (EcoRI, BamHI, HindIII, and friends) intersected with the enzymes that actually cut this molecule. Unique cutter...

When you settle on a selection you want to reuse, the Saved sets control lets you name it and keep it. Saved sets are stored per user and follow you across every sequence you open, so a panel of go-to enzymes is one click away the next time, not something you rebuild by hand.

The restriction digest and the Cloning Workspace are two views of the same enzyme model. Use the digest to find the cutters and overhangs you want, then carry the same enzymes into a restriction-ligation or Golden Gate assembly in the Cloning Workspace to build the construct. ...

This page used to be one section of the Sequences workbench page. The enzyme catalog and digest tools grew enough depth to stand on their own, so they live here now. The digest still runs inside the Sequences editor, on whatever sequence you have open.

Where an enzyme cuts is a question with one correct answer, so the cut detection is not an approximation. The digest is validated band-for-band against Biopython's module, the standard reference for restriction analysis, and that comparison runs as part of the test suite and i...

Cut sites overlaid on the plasmid map once the Enzyme menu's Cut sites toggle is on. Each label points a leader line at the exact cut position, and a count next to an enzyme (XhoI x8) shows how many times it cuts.

A circular plasmid map of pEGFP-N1 with restriction cut sites overlaid as labeled tick marks around the ring (NcoI, XhoI, NotI, SphI and others), each with a leader line to its cut position, shown beside the base-level sequence.

entries.title: Where results live now
headings: Per-project completed work → project Results gallery
bodySnippets: ResearchOS used to have a standalone Results page that listed every completed task, grouped by project. That page was retired. Completed work now surfaces in three places, each closer to the context where the work happens.

Experiments that you've marked complete now collect in the Earlier results section at the bottom of the Workbench page, with the header rendered in uppercase as EARLIER RESULTS . They keep all the same write-up affordances. Clicking a card opens the task detail popup on its De...

Because this archive grows for as long as you keep working, it paginates instead of dumping everything at once. The flat view shows a page of cards with a Show more button for the rest. The by-project view collapses each project group (tap a group to expand it) and caps a long...
Finished purchase orders appear inline in the Purchases chronological list alongside active orders, marked with a green dot and a text suffix. There is no separate Earlier section.

Clicking a project card on the Home page opens that project's detail popup (the deep link lives at ). Its Go to section has a Results doorway that opens a gallery of every result image for that project. This is the fastest way to review a project's result images without leavin...

Visiting now redirects to Workbench , where the bulk of the old page's content lives. No data moved on disk, every task still lives at . New file attachments land in per-tab subdirectories. Notes files go to and results files go to , rather than a flat folder at the task root.
is queued for recapture to reflect the current Workbench layout.

The standalone Results page was a fourth surface that mostly duplicated work the per-feature pages were already doing better. Folding completion into Workbench, Purchases, and the project popup keeps each completion view closer to the live work it belongs to, instead of forcin...
The Earlier results section sits at the bottom of the Workbench. The project-grouping toggle in its header flips between flat and project-grouped views.
The Earlier results section at the bottom of the Workbench page, with completed experiment cards grouped by project.

entries.title: Search
headings: What gets searched, and what doesn't
bodySnippets: A filter-driven search page that finds tasks across every project under your user. Fill in the form, click Search, click a result card to open its task popup.

The page opens with an empty filter card and a placeholder that reads "Search to get started" over "Enter keywords, dates, or filters above." Type into the Keywords box (or use any of the dropdowns), then click the blue Search button. Results render below as a grid of cards, o...
You can also land here pre-loaded. Choosing "Search everything for" in the BeakerSearch palette opens , which seeds the Keywords box and runs the search automatically.
Every filter is optional. Leave them blank to match everything, or combine as many as you want. All filters AND together.

Each card carries a colored stripe matching its project, the task name, a project pill, a task-type pill (purple for experiments, amber for purchases, gray for list tasks), the schedule and duration, the method (when one is attached), and the first few tags. A green Complete b...

The Search page doubles as the launch pad for bulk exports. Click the Select button next to the result count and the cards sprout a checkbox in the top-right corner. Tick one or more result cards (a blue ring and the checked mark show selection), and the header swaps in a coun...

Clicking Export selected opens the same Export dialog the experiment popup uses. It offers PDF report , HTML report , and Raw ResearchOS format , plus a Combined PDF option (only when you pick more than one) that merges every selected experiment into one navigable PDF with a c...
See Export an experiment on the Experiments page for a deeper walkthrough of what each format bundles and which one to pick.
Keywords matches the task name, its tags, and the name and tags of its first attached method. Multiple words AND together (e.g., only returns tasks where both words appear).
Date From and Date To restrict to tasks whose schedule overlaps the range.
Task Type narrows to one of the three task shapes (i.e., Experiments, Purchases, or List Tasks).
Project limits results to tasks inside one project.
Specific Method matches tasks whose first attached method is the one you pick. Method Category is the broader version and matches on the method's folder.
Completion Status filters to complete or incomplete tasks.
PDF packages each experiment as its own selectable-text PDF with table of contents and outline pane, then drops every PDF into a single .
HTML gives each experiment its own subfolder inside the zip (one self-contained HTML page plus an tree), so you can open each report independently without an extra unzip step.
Raw nests one per experiment inside the outer zip, keeping each bundle ready to drop into another ResearchOS user's Settings → Import experiment dialog.
Pressing Enter while focused on the Keywords box runs the search without needing to click the button.

Keywords match against task names, task tags, and method names and tags; they do not match against note bodies, results text, or PCR protocol contents. Open the Notes page or a method's own editor to search the inside of those files.
The Search page. Type into Keywords, narrow with the filters, then click Search.
The Search page with a keywords input, a row of filter dropdowns, and Search and Clear buttons.
Select mode on Search. Tick the cards you want and click Export selected to open the format dialog.
Search results in select mode: three cards ticked with blue rings and the Export selected button visible at the top of the results list.

entries.title: Sequences
headings: The tab bar and view modes
Connection to the rest of the app
bodySnippets: The Sequences workbench is where plasmids, oligos, genes, and protein sequences live alongside your experiments and notes.
